title: Machine Learning-assisted Antenna Design Optimization: A Review and the State-of-the-art
ai_technique: Review of GP/kriging surrogate models, SADEA (surrogate-assisted differential evolution), space mapping, multi-fidelity kriging
core_claim: A review article summarizes the current state of machine-learning-assisted antenna optimization methods (surrogate-based optimization), focusing on their efficiency and optimization capability for single-objective, multi-objective, multi-fidelity, and yield-driven tasks.
results: This is a review with no original experiments; from the cited literature, the authors report that the SADEA family of algorithms (surrogate model-assisted differential evolution) achieves a 3- to 20-fold speedup over standard global optimization methods.
limitations: Not stated explicitly as a limitation of the work itself (this is a review); the authors merely recommend, for future use, methods suited to high-dimensional spaces without requiring an initial design as generally the most robust.
source_file: old/141 - Machine Learning-assisted Antenna Design optimization A Review and the State-of-the-art.pdf

title: Fast Multi-Objective Optimization of Multi-Parameter Antenna Structures Based on Improved BPNN Surrogate Model
ai_technique: SC-BPNN surrogate model tuned by hybrid real-binary PSO (HPSO)
core_claim: The authors propose a sparsely connected backpropagation neural network (SC-BPNN) as a surrogate model, whose structure and weights are tuned by a hybrid real-binary PSO (HPSO), and combine it with a multi-objective evolutionary algorithm for fast optimization of multiple antenna parameters.
results: The proposed SC-BPNN+HPSO surrogate model achieves higher accuracy in predicting antenna behavior and significantly lower computational cost (CPU time) than earlier approaches; demonstrated on the Pareto-optimal design of a compact tri-band planar monopole antenna.
limitations: The authors state that the computational cost of the proposed method is slightly higher than that of a simpler comparison method (scheme II), even though the prediction accuracy is significantly higher.
source_file: old/120 - Fast Multi-Objective Optimization of Multi-Parameter Antenna Structures Based on Improved BPNN Surrogate Model.pdf

title: Machine Learning in Antenna Design: An Overview on Machine Learning Concept and Algorithms
ai_technique: Review of ML algorithms (regression, ANN, SVM, KNN, k-means); example: SADEA (GPR + differential evolution)
core_claim: A review article presents the fundamental concepts of machine learning (supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning) and their algorithms, focusing mainly on their use in antenna design, including a comparison with conventional design methods.
results: No original quantitative results; as an example from the literature, it cites the SADEA method (Gaussian process regression + differential evolution), which accelerated the design and optimization of three antennas by more than 4× compared to differential evolution alone.
limitations: In the conclusion, the authors identify general challenges of ML in antenna design: selecting a suitable algorithm, problem formulation, obtaining a sufficient amount of training data (requiring many EM simulations), and data preprocessing.
source_file: old/134 - Machine Learning in Antenna Design An Overview on Machine Learning Concept and Algorithms.pdf

title: Software Solutions for Antenna Design Exploration: A Comparison of Packages, Tools, Techniques, and Algorithms for Various Design Challenges
ai_technique: Comparison of SADEA (surrogate-assisted DE) with PSO, DE, CMA-ES, TRF, N-M, SQP, IQN, PS
core_claim: The article compares 17 optimization methods (in six categories) implemented in antenna design tools (HFSS, CST, FEKO, MATLAB, ADE) on three representative design problems.
results: SADEA achieves the highest quality of results with the fewest EM simulations across all three test problems; CMA-ES, PSO, and DE are less efficient despite comparable global search capability.
limitations: The performance of the PS, SQP, TRF, N-M, and IQN methods strongly depends on the quality of the initial design; the authors note that the conclusions are based on three selected problems and may not fully generalize to all antenna problems.
source_file: old/44 - Software Solutions for Antenna Design Exploration A Comparison of Packages, Tools, Techniques, and Algorithms for Various Design Challenges.pdf

title: Performance-Based Nested Surrogate Modeling of Antenna Input Characteristics
ai_technique: Two-level (nested) kriging surrogate model
core_claim: The authors propose a two-level (nested) kriging surrogate model for antenna input characteristics, in which the first level defines the domain of the second (final) level of the model, enabling modeling over wide ranges of geometric parameters and performance quantities.
results: Demonstrated on two microstrip antennas, with a favorable comparison against conventional kriging and RBF interpolation; experimental validation on fabricated prototypes showed good agreement between simulated and measured reflection characteristics, with minor frequency shifts.
limitations: The authors state that the minor differences (frequency shifts) between simulation and measurement are caused by the fact that SMA connectors were not included in the antenna's computational model.
source_file: old/132 - Performance-Based Nested Surrogate Modeling of Antenna Input Characteristics.pdf

title: An Analysis of Metaheuristic Algorithms Used for the Recovery of a Failed Antenna Element in an Antenna Array
ai_technique: Comparison of metaheuristics: Firefly, Bat, Flower Pollination, Cuckoo Search, Harmony Search, Simulated Annealing, Gradient Descent
core_claim: This work compares seven metaheuristic algorithms (gradient descent, simulated annealing, firefly, bat, flower pollination, cuckoo search, harmony search) for recovering the weight of a failed element in the antenna array of a GB-SAR system.
results: Of all the tested methods, the firefly algorithm achieves the best results in finding the weight of a failed main element in a 2x6 array (a specific numerical error/accuracy value is not given in the available excerpt).
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the study is limited to a specific 2x6 array geometry and a single-element failure scenario; generalization to multiple failures or other array configurations is not discussed in the text.
source_file: old/65 - An Analysis of Metaheuristic Algorithms Used for the Recovery of a Failed Antenna Element in an Antenna Array.pdf

title: Electromagnetic Machine Learning for Inverse Modeling Using the Spatial Singularity Expansion Method
ai_technique: Spatial Singularity Expansion Method (S-SEM) + ML inverse source model
core_claim: The authors propose a unified EM-ML approach combining the spatial singularity expansion method (S-SEM) with machine learning for inverse modeling and characterization of antennas, enabling reconstruction of current distribution and geometric parameters of radiators from far-field data.
results: The method successfully reconstructed the parameters (length, orientation) of straight and bent wire antennas and their fields; validation on a fabricated half-wave dipole antenna (1 GHz) in an anechoic chamber showed good agreement between the estimated S-SEM parameters and orientation and measurement, with some deviation in the wire length estimate.
limitations: The authors state that the deviation in wire length reconstruction is caused by the fact that the exact formula was derived for an ideal thin-wire radiation model, which may not fully hold for the fabricated probe; increasing the S-SEM order improves reconstruction but significantly slows convergence.
source_file: old/219 - Electromagnetic Machine Learning for Inverse Modeling Using the Spatial Singularity Expansion Method.pdf

title: A Modified Efficient KNN Method for Antenna Optimization and Design
ai_technique: Modified KNN regression with self-learning (active) training-data augmentation
core_claim: The authors propose a modified KNN regression method with a self-learning loop that generates additional training data on its own during antenna optimization using a simulator (HFSS), thereby requiring only a very small initial dataset.
results: The method is 5–30× faster than traditional ML methods (ANN, Bayesian optimization); for a three-parameter antenna, only 8 training samples are needed, and for a nine-parameter model, 30 samples; validated on four different antenna examples.
limitations: The authors state that future work should focus on multi-objective optimization, since the number of training samples required grows with the number of objectives and parameters.
source_file: old/124 - A Modified Efficient KNN Method for Antenna Optimization and Design.pdf

title: A review on the design and optimization of antennas using machine learning algorithms and techniques
ai_technique: Review of regression ML models (SVR, GPR, ANN, etc.) for antenna design and optimization
core_claim: A comprehensive literature review describing the use of machine learning in antenna design and optimization, including an overview of computational electromagnetics, learning categories, and regression ML models applied to antenna synthesis and analysis.
results: No original quantitative results; this is an extensive review (28 pages) organized by antenna type, summarizing dozens of works using ANN, SVR, GPR, and other regression ML models to predict antenna characteristics.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors generally mention that the success of ML approaches strongly depends on the quality, quantity, and availability of training data, which must be obtained through simulations because standardized antenna datasets are lacking.
source_file: old/119 - A review on the design and optimization of antennas using machine learning algorithms and techniques.pdf

title: Antenna Design Exploration and Optimization using Machine Learning
ai_technique: RBF neural network (surrogate regression) + PCA (dimensionality reduction) + genetic algorithm/MOGA (evolutionary learning)
core_claim: The authors show how combining design-space exploration (DoE) with machine learning techniques (RBF neural networks, PCA, evolutionary learning) can substantially accelerate the process of antenna performance analysis and optimization, on three examples with different design- and response-space dimensions.
results: For the blade antenna (p=4, n=4), the RBF regression model reduced the evaluation time from 90 s (simulation) to 20 ms (speedup of approx. 45,000×) with R² up to 0.9999 and median relative error of 0.03–5.3%; for the LTE dual antenna, the ML approach reduced the total optimization time to under 15 hours compared to 40.4 hours for classical optimization; evolutionary learning for a WLAN antenna with 112 binary variables reached, after approx. 4300 simulations, a solution with S11 < −10 dB in both bands (2.44 and 5.2 GHz).
limitations: The authors state that for high-dimensional discrete design spaces (2^122 combinations for the WLAN antenna), building regression models is computationally very demanding due to the discreteness and strong nonlinearity of the mapping, which is why evolutionary learning must be used instead of classical regression.
source_file: old/148 - Antenna Design Exploration and Optimization using Machine Learning.pdf

title: Expedited Yield Optimization of Narrow- and Multi-Band Antennas Using Performance-Driven Surrogates
ai_technique: Performance-driven surrogate model defined by reference (pre-optimized) designs for yield optimization
core_claim: The authors propose a simple surrogate technique for fast yield optimization of narrowband and multi-band antennas, in which the optimization domain is defined using several pre-optimized reference designs representing the main directions of resonant-frequency variation.
results: Verified on a ring-slot antenna and a two- and three-band uniplanar dipole antenna; optimization was achieved at a cost of only 62, 74, and 132 EM simulations, respectively; the reliability of the results was confirmed by comparison with EM Monte Carlo simulations.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available text (introduction and reference conclusion) contains no separate discussion of limitations; the method generally requires a set of pre-optimized reference designs as input.
source_file: old/128 - Expedited Yield Optimization of Narrow- and Multi-Band Antennas Using Performance-Driven Surrogates.pdf

title: Machine-learning-assisted optimization and its application to antenna designs: Opportunities and challenges
ai_technique: Review of MLAO methods: GPR, SVM, ANN as surrogate models for antenna design
core_claim: A review article describes ML-assisted optimization (MLAO) of antennas as a type of surrogate-based optimization, in which an ML model (GPR, SVM, ANN) replaces computationally expensive EM simulations, and summarizes algorithms for antenna optimization and sensitivity analysis (SA).
results: No original quantitative results; this is a review summarizing the state of MLAO methods for antenna modeling and discussing their algorithms.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the text does not specify concrete limitations of individual methods, only discussing future MLAO challenges in general terms.
source_file: old/125 - Machine-learning-assisted optimization and its application to antenna designs Opportunities and challenges.pdf

title: Multistage Collaborative Machine Learning and its Application to Antenna Modeling and Optimization
ai_technique: Multi-stage collaboration of SOGPR + symmetric and asymmetric MOGPR (Gaussian process regression)
core_claim: The authors propose a multi-stage collaborative machine learning method (MS-CoML) combining single-output (SOGPR) and symmetric/asymmetric multi-output (MOGPR) Gaussian process regression for efficient multi-objective modeling and optimization of antennas with variable-fidelity data.
results: On three antenna examples (a single-band, a wideband, and a multi-band antenna in the Q, V, and S/C bands), the method significantly reduced total optimization time while maintaining high prediction accuracy, using only a small proportion of high-fidelity (HF) data; specific numerical accuracy/speedup values are not given in the available text excerpt.
limitations: In the conclusion, the authors explicitly state that the limitations of the proposed MS-CoML method were discussed, but their specific wording is not present in the available text excerpt; not stated explicitly: this likely relates to the dependence of accuracy on sufficient correlation between the variable-fidelity data and the individual design tasks.
source_file: old/139 - Multistage Collaborative Machine Learning and its Application to Antenna Modeling and Optimization.pdf

title: Rapid Multi-Criterial Antenna Optimization by Means of Pareto Front Triangulation and Interpolative Design Predictors
ai_technique: Pareto front triangulation + local interpolative (kriging-like) surrogate models
core_claim: The authors propose a deterministic ML procedure for multi-objective (Pareto) antenna optimization that sequentially generates Pareto-optimal designs using triangulation of the Pareto front obtained so far and local surrogate models.
results: The method enables the generation of uniformly distributed Pareto fronts at a cost on the order of hundreds of antenna EM simulations; verified on three validation case studies and compared with current multi-objective optimization techniques.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available excerpt has no separate section on limitations; the method is sensitive to high dimensionality and strong nonlinearity of antenna characteristics, as mentioned in the introduction as a general problem of the field.
source_file: old/129 - Rapid Multi-Criterial Antenna Optimization by Means of Pareto Front Triangulation and Interpolative Design Predictors.pdf

title: Deep Learning Based Antenna Design and Beam-Steering Capabilities for Millimeter-Wave Applications
ai_technique: DNN (5-layer network) + hybrid MGSA-PSO optimization of hyperparameters and beam-steering
core_claim: The authors implement a deep neural network (DNN) to compute the resonant frequency of a dual-band circularly polarized patch antenna (BSPA) and for beam-steering of a circular antenna array using a hybrid MGSA-PSO algorithm.
results: A database of 150 BSPA samples was split into training (135) and test (15) sets; the 16-element beam-steering array achieved a sidelobe level ≤ -30 dB at the tested angles (40°, 142°, 205°, 320°) with good agreement between the desired and synthesized radiation patterns.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the work does not discuss the computational requirements of training the hybrid MGSA-PSO algorithm, nor the sensitivity of the results to the size of the training database (only 150 samples).
source_file: old/166 - Deep Learning Based Antenna Design and Beam-Steering Capabilities for Millimeter-Wave Applications.pdf

title: Development of a High Gain FSS Reflector Backed Monopole Antenna Using Machine Learning for 5G Applications
ai_technique: Artificial neural network (ANN) with backpropagation, 100 neurons in the hidden layer
core_claim: The authors develop a high-gain monopole antenna backed by a frequency-selective surface (FSS) reflector for 5G applications, and use an artificial neural network (ANN) to find the optimal position of the FSS layer and the ground-plane dimension.
results: The proposed antenna (50 × 50 mm on Rogers 5880 substrate) operates in the 5.53–6.36 GHz band (targeting 6 GHz) for 5G Sub-6GHz applications; integration of the FSS layer provides a low-profile antenna with higher gain and radiation efficiency than comparable designs from the literature; the ANN model used 75% of the data for training, 10% for validation, and 15% for testing, with 100 neurons in the hidden layer.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available excerpt does not contain a conclusion; a likely limitation is that no fixed analytical formulas exist for this composite structure, and the validity of the ML prediction is limited to the range of the training data.
source_file: old/156 - Development of a High Gain FSS Reflector Backed Monopole Antenna Using Machine Learning for 5G Applications.pdf

title: Machine Learning-Enabled Joint Antenna Selection and Precoding Design: From Offline Complexity to Online Performance
ai_technique: Deep neural network (DNN) for antenna selection prediction and precoder design
core_claim: The authors propose the L-ASPD algorithm, which uses a deep neural network to learn the relationship between system parameters and selected antennas as a computationally lightweight alternative to the combinatorially complex JASPD algorithm for joint antenna selection and precoder design in multi-user MIMO downlink systems.
results: L-ASPD significantly outperforms baseline methods (block diagonalization, learning-assisted broadcasting solutions) in effective sum data rate under limited computation time, and reduces computational complexity by 95% while retaining more than 95% of the optimal performance.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available excerpt does not contain a conclusion (only author biographies); a likely limitation is the dependence of DNN performance on the quality and scope of training data, and sensitivity to changes in user/channel distribution outside the training range.
source_file: old/149 - Machine Learning-Enabled Joint Antenna Selection and Precoding Design From Offline Complexity to Online Performance.pdf

title: Multilayer Machine Learning-Assisted Optimization-Based Robust Design and Its Applications to Antennas and Array
ai_technique: GPR/ANN surrogate models + genetic algorithm (worst-case analysis) within a multi-layer MLAO framework
core_claim: The authors propose a multi-layer ML-assisted optimization method (ML-MLAO) for robust antenna and array design, which integrates surrogate models into worst-case analysis, MITH search, and robust optimization.
results: The method was validated on a series-fed array of 10 microstrip elements at 5.8 GHz; accurate prediction of the Pareto front and MITH was achieved while satisfying |S11|<-14 dB and SLL<-18 dB after four iterations, with significantly lower computational cost than conventional robust-design optimization methods.
limitations: Not stated explicitly in the available text (a concluding section is missing); the method requires a two-level nested iteration loop, and the accuracy of MITH prediction depends on the quality of the surrogate model in the early iterations.
source_file: old/160 - Multilayer Machine Learning-Assisted Optimization-Based Robust Design and Its Applications to Antennas and Array.pdf

title: Artificial Intelligence for Adaptive and Reconfigurable Antenna Arrays: A Review
ai_technique: Review of ANN/DNN/CNN, SVM, and GA across adaptive array applications
core_claim: AI algorithms (ANN/DNN, SVM, GA, etc.) are more robust than classical signal-processing algorithms in noisy and multipath environments and enable more efficient adaptive and reconfigurable control of antenna arrays across five key application areas.
results: A review article with no original quantitative results; it summarizes the use of ANN/DNN/CNN, SVM, and GA in adaptive nulling, wireless localization, MIMO communication, element failure detection, and array calibration, citing dozens of experimental papers (e.g., DNN dominates CSI estimation for MIMO, GA for calibration and failure detection).
limitations: Not stated explicitly as a methodological limitation: the authors merely note that AI algorithms are constrained by the rules and skills of their code developers and that human oversight of their decision-making is necessary.
source_file: old/100 - Artificial Intelligence for Adaptive and Reconfigurable Antenna Arrays A Review.pdf

title: Optimized Two-Level Ensemble Model for Predicting the Parameters of Metamaterial Antenna
ai_technique: Two-level ensemble (RF, SVR, LightGBM + ElasticNet) tuned by ASSOA
core_claim: A two-level ensemble regression model (RF, SVR, LightGBM at the first level + ElasticNet at the second level) tuned by the ASSOA metaheuristic can predict the bandwidth of a metamaterial antenna more accurately than previously published ensemble models.
results: The proposed model achieved an RMSE of 0.013, MAE of 0.004, and MBE of 0.0017 when predicting the bandwidth of a metamaterial antenna on a public benchmark dataset, outperforming the competing ensemble models compared.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors merely state the superiority of their results over other models, without discussing specific limitations of the approach.
source_file: old/112 - Optimized Two-Level Ensemble Model for Predicting the Parameters of Metamaterial Antenna.pdf

title: Multibranch Machine Learning-Assisted Optimization and Its Application to Antenna Design
ai_technique: Multi-fidelity Gaussian process regression (GPR) with an adaptive lower confidence bound (LCB) strategy and multi-branch search
core_claim: The authors propose a multi-branch machine-learning-assisted optimization method (MB-MLAO) with an adaptive variable lower confidence bound (LCB) and retraining of a multi-fidelity GPR model for efficient antenna optimization and worst-case performance (WCP) search under manufacturing tolerances.
results: The method was demonstrated on two test functions and three antenna types, where the combination of multi-fidelity GPR and multi-branch adaptive LCB search ensures a balance between exploitation and exploration; specific numerical accuracy/speedup values are not given in the available text excerpt.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available excerpt does not contain a concluding discussion of limitations; a likely limitation is the dependence of method performance on the choice of LCB constants and on the quality of low-fidelity (LF) data.
source_file: old/142 - Multibranch Machine Learning-Assisted Optimization and Its Application to Antenna Design.pdf

title: Design of Antennas through Artificial Intelligence: State of the Art and Challenges
ai_technique: Review of evolutionary algorithms (GA, PSO, DE) and ML surrogate models for antenna design
core_claim: A review article summarizes applications of AI (evolutionary algorithms, machine learning, knowledge representation via ontologies) in antenna design and identifies challenges and future research directions.
results: No original quantitative results; this is a review summarizing the use of GA, PSO, DE, and other evolutionary algorithms, ML as surrogate models in conjunction with optimizers, and OWL ontologies in the antenna domain.
limitations: The authors state that the large volume of data required by most ML methods is a major obstacle, and that there is no unified way to select a suitable optimization algorithm for a specific antenna problem; they propose meta-learning and GAN-based data generation as future directions.
source_file: old/116 - Design of Antennas through Artificial Intelligence State of the Art and Challenges.pdf

title: Dual Band Antenna Design and Prediction of Resonance Frequency Using Machine Learning Approaches
ai_technique: CNN, linear/ridge/lasso regression, random forest, decision tree, XGBoost regression (best: DTR)
core_claim: The authors design a dual-band microstrip patch antenna (MPA) and evaluate it using a combination of simulation, measurement, an RLC equivalent circuit, and seven ML models for resonant-frequency prediction.
results: The best results were achieved by the DTR (decision tree regression) model: MSE = 0.71%, MAE = 5.63%, RMSE = 8.42%, variance score = 99.68%; the antenna has a measured bandwidth of 6.7 GHz and a simulated bandwidth of 5.9 GHz within the 7.9–14.6 GHz range (X-band and lower Ku-band).
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available excerpt contains only the abstract and introduction, the conclusion is missing; the likely limitation is the small dataset (86 samples) and restriction to a single antenna/substrate type.
source_file: old/140 - Dual Band Antenna Design and Prediction of Resonance Frequency Using Machine Learning Approaches.pdf

title: Estimation of S11 Values of Patch Antenna Using Various Machine Learning Models
ai_technique: Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost, KNN regression
core_claim: The authors show that machine learning (Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost, KNN) can be used to predict the S11 (return loss) of a microstrip patch antenna based on dimensions A, B and frequency, without the need for repeated HFSS simulations.
results: On a dataset of 45,551 samples, the KNN model achieved the best results with MAE=1.328, MSE=4.608, and R²=0.866; the predicted and HFSS-simulated S11 values for 4 designs were very close (e.g., -26.439 dB vs. -23.966 dB at 6.32 GHz).
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the work does not evaluate the model's generalization to other antenna types/substrates or its sensitivity to noise in the data; the conclusion merely states the prediction accuracy without discussing limitations.
source_file: old/159 - Estimation of S11 Values of Patch Antenna Using Various Machine Learning Models.pdf

title: Low-cost quasi-global optimization of expensive electromagnetic simulation models by inverse surrogates and response features
ai_technique: PSO + inverse regression surrogate with response features
core_claim: The authors propose a globalized search method for tuning high-frequency structures (antennas, microwave couplers, impedance transformers) combining response features with inverse regression surrogate models, aiming to achieve a cost comparable to local optimization procedures.
results: The global search capability and computational efficiency of the method are demonstrated by comparison with multi-start local search and with a particle swarm optimizer on several high-frequency structures; the total optimization cost is comparable to that of purely local procedures (specific numbers of EM simulations are not further specified in the available text excerpt).
limitations: The authors explicitly state that the approach requires problem-specific routines for identifying characteristic points in the response, which limits its direct applicability to structures where such points can be reliably distinguished.
source_file: old/271 - Low-cost quasi-global optimization of expensive electromagnetic simulation models by inverse surrogates and response features.pdf

title: Unequally Spaced Antenna Array Synthesis Using Accelerating Gaussian Mutated Cat Swarm Optimization
ai_technique: Accelerating Gaussian Mutated Cat Swarm Optimization (AGMCSO)
core_claim: The authors propose a nature-inspired metaheuristic algorithm, AGMCSO (Accelerating Gaussian-Mutated Cat Swarm Optimization), for suppressing peak sidelobe level (PSLL) in the synthesis of non-uniformly spaced linear antenna arrays.
results: On benchmark multimodal problems, AGMCSO exhibits higher convergence speed and accuracy than conventional algorithms, and in the synthesis of non-uniformly spaced linear arrays it achieves a lower PSLL with a narrower first-null beamwidth (FNBW) than GA, PSO, DE, ACO, CSO, and GWO.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available text (introduction and references) contains no separate discussion of limitations of the proposed algorithm.
source_file: old/133 - Unequally Spaced Antenna Array Synthesis Using Accelerating Gaussian Mutated Cat Swarm Optimization.pdf

title: Bayesian Optimization Based on Student's T Process for Microstrip Antenna Design
ai_technique: Bayesian optimization with a Student's T-process (BO-STP-EST)
core_claim: Bayesian optimization with a Student's T-process as the surrogate model (instead of a Gaussian process), combined with an improved EST acquisition function, offers more robust and accurate global optimization for microstrip antenna resonant frequency design.
results: On a multimodal test function, BO-STP-EST finds the global minimum, while models with a GP surrogate (BO-GP-LCB/EST) get stuck in a local minimum; for a printed dipole antenna, it reached the target (2.45 GHz, S11 = −24.12 dB) already at the 3rd iteration (versus the 8th–41st iteration for the other models), and for an E-shaped antenna (3.50 GHz) at the 7th iteration versus the 12th–42nd iteration for the other models.
limitations: The authors explicitly state that in this work the algorithm was applied only to single-band antenna optimization; optimization of multi-band antennas is left for future work.
source_file: old/113 - Bayesian Optimization Based on Student’s T Process for Microstrip Antenna Design.pdf

title: Machine learning assisted metamaterial-based reconfigurable antenna for low-cost portable electronic devices
ai_technique: Extra Tree Regression (ExTRM)
core_claim: The authors propose a reconfigurable metamaterial antenna (patch with SRR and thin-wire superstrates) switched by PIN diodes for 5G/WiFi/WiMAX/WLAN applications, and use an ML model to speed up reflectance prediction and shorten simulation time.
results: The best design variant (two layers of TW superstrate) achieves a gain of 15.57 dB, bandwidth of 108 MHz, reflectance of −27.07 dB, and six frequency bands; the Extra Tree Regression (ExTRM) model reduced simulation time and requirements by 80%.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available excerpt does not contain a separate limitations section; a likely limitation is that the ML prediction is valid only within the parameter range used for training and for the given antenna geometry.
source_file: old/145 - Machine learning assisted metamaterial-based reconfigurable antenna for low-cost portable electronic devices.pdf

title: Low-cost yield-driven design of antenna structures using response-variability essential directions and parameter space reduction
ai_technique: Surrogate model with parameter-space reduction via essential directions (response-variability)
core_claim: The authors propose a low-cost surrogate method for antenna manufacturing yield optimization that restricts the metamodel domain to a few 'essential directions' that most affect the antenna response, thereby reducing the cost of training data.
results: Verified on four microstrip antennas; the average cost of yield optimization amounts to just 115 EM simulations, with a speedup of up to 88% over a simple surrogate method and 59% over sequential approximation optimization (SAO), at comparable resulting yield quality.
limitations: The authors point out that physical measurements of antenna prototypes are subject to errors whose magnitude is typically greater than the antenna's response variability due to manufacturing tolerances, which complicates experimental validation.
source_file: old/121 - Low-cost yield-driven design of antenna structures using response-variability essential directions and parameter space reduction.pdf

title: Ultra-Wideband CPW Fed Band-Notched Monopole Antenna Optimization Using Machine Learning
ai_technique: Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost Regression, KNN, ANN (best: KNN, 98% accuracy)
core_claim: The authors propose a compact CPW-fed UWB monopole antenna with a band-notch, and optimize the prediction of its reflection coefficient using five ML algorithms (Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost, KNN, ANN).
results: The fabricated antenna covers the 2.9–21.6 GHz band with a 3.2–3.9 GHz band-notch (WiMAX); the KNN algorithm achieved the best results among the compared models, with accuracy up to 98%.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available excerpt contains only a summary conclusion without a discussion of limitations; a likely limitation is that the prediction is valid only within the range of parameters (slot length/width/thickness) used in the simulation for the given antenna geometry.
source_file: old/158 - Ultra-Wideband CPW Fed Band-Notched Monopole Antenna Optimization Using Machine Learning.pdf

title: An Intelligent Antenna Synthesis Method Based on Machine Learning
ai_technique: SVM antenna-type classification + stacking ensemble model (five base learners) for parameter prediction
core_claim: The authors propose an intelligent antenna synthesis system that uses SVM classification to automatically recommend a suitable antenna type and a stacking ensemble model to provide the corresponding geometric parameters based on required performance characteristics.
results: The system achieves an antenna-type classification accuracy above 99% and predicts geometric parameters with a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) below 6%, with better accuracy than a traditional data-driven model; demonstrated on a microstrip patch, spiral, and horn antenna.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the text contains no separate discussion of the method's limitations beyond the accuracy comparison tables.
source_file: old/136 - An Intelligent Antenna Synthesis Method Based on Machine Learning.pdf

title: Microstrip line fed dielectric resonator antenna optimization using machine learning algorithms
ai_technique: ANN, KNN, Random Forest, XGBoost, Decision Tree; the ANN accuracy problem addressed using a Knowledge-Based Neural Network (KBNN)
core_claim: The authors, for the first time, optimize a microstrip-fed dielectric resonator antenna (DRA) using several ML algorithms (ANN, KNN, Random Forest, XGBoost, Decision Tree) and introduce a Knowledge-Based Neural Network (KBNN) to address ANN accuracy issues.
results: The predictions of all ML models were close to the measured values, with the exception of the standalone ANN, whose problem was resolved by deploying KBNN; the antenna operates in the 3.3–3.65 GHz band with a gain of approx. 3.5 dBi, and the measured results agree well with the simulation (resonant frequency predicted by the empirical formula is 3.35 GHz, close to the simulated value).
limitations: The authors state that neural networks (ANN) require more computation time due to their layered structure and complex learning algorithm, and that the KBNN (PKI) model required the most training time because it needed a larger dataset to achieve accuracy.
source_file: old/153 - Microstrip line fed dielectric resonator antenna optimization using machine learning algorithms.pdf

title: Electromagnetic characteristic estimation on spiral antennas through AOI, ML, and AI
ai_technique: Gaussian process regression (GPR) with exponential covariance function
core_claim: The authors propose a method combining automatic optical inspection (AOI), machine learning, and AI for estimating the electromagnetic characteristics (coupling frequency) of spiral antennas based on line edge roughness (LER) of the pattern, enabling smart manufacturing.
results: An AI model based on Gaussian process regression with an exponential covariance function achieved, on the testing group (36 samples), an integrated RMSE of 0.010989 GHz and a coefficient of determination R² = 0.904 between predicted and measured coupling frequency.
limitations: The authors state that the degraded RMSE in the testing group was caused by deviation in samples with BA = 0.45 and unbalanced clusters of samples on one side of the reference line; with a sufficient number of samples (which was not practically feasible in this work), the RMSE would, according to the authors, improve.
source_file: old/218 - Electromagnetic characteristic estimation on spiral antennas through AOI, ML, and AI.pdf

title: Design of Zero Clearance SIW Endfire Antenna Array Using Machine Learning-Assisted Optimization
ai_technique: PSADEA (parallel surrogate-model-assisted hybrid differential evolution)
core_claim: The authors propose a substrate-integrated waveguide (SIW) antenna array with zero clearance for 5G mmWave applications, whose impedance matching across three metal vias is optimized using the ML method PSADEA (parallel surrogate-model-assisted hybrid differential evolution).
results: The optimized antenna covers the 36–40 GHz band with overall efficiency generally above 60% and peak gain above 5 dBi (measured 4.27 dBi for the single antenna and 3.7 dBi for the array element); the beam-steering range at 39 GHz is from −20° to 35°; the PSADEA method achieves more than a 20-fold optimization speedup over standard methods (PSO, DE, GA).
limitations: Not stated explicitly as a separate section; the text indicates that agreement between simulation and measurement is affected by fabrication location errors.
source_file: old/150 - Design of Zero Clearance SIW Endfire Antenna Array Using Machine Learning-Assisted Optimization.pdf

title: A Machine Learning Generative Method for Automating Antenna Design and Optimization
ai_technique: GAN-inspired generative method (discriminator + generator) with an evolutionary selection criterion
core_claim: The authors propose a generative machine learning method (GAN-inspired discriminators and generators) for automating the design and optimization of antennas with a flexible geometric scheme of an interconnected node network of arbitrary shape.
results: For the design of a dual-resonance antenna, the proposed method outperformed other state-of-the-art optimization algorithms; besides finding optimal solutions, the trained generator can also be used to automate future antenna designs. Specific numerical speedup/accuracy values are not given in the available text excerpt.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available excerpt does not contain a conclusion (only references); a likely limitation is the dependence of generator quality on discriminator accuracy and limited testing on a single type of geometric scheme.

title: Machine learning-based technique for gain and resonance prediction of mid band 5G Yagi antenna
ai_technique: Nine regression ML models (best: linear regression for frequency, Gaussian process regression for gain)
core_claim: The authors investigate the use of an ML technique to improve the performance of a Quasi-Yagi-Uda antenna in the n78 band for 5G applications, with evaluation via simulation, measurement, and an RLC equivalent circuit.
results: The antenna achieves an operating frequency of 3.5 GHz, return loss of −43.45 dB, bandwidth of 520 MHz, maximum gain of 6.57 dB, and efficiency of nearly 97%; of nine ML models, linear regression achieved the best results for resonant frequency prediction and Gaussian process regression for gain prediction, both with R²/variance score close to 99%.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available excerpt does not contain a concluding discussion of limitations; a likely limitation is the limited range of simulation-generated training data and the focus on only one antenna type.

title: Machine learning-based technique for resonance and directivity prediction of UMTS LTE band quasi Yagi antenna
ai_technique: Seven regression ML models (best: linear regression for directivity, ridge regression for frequency)
core_claim: The authors propose a quasi-Yagi-Uda antenna for the UMTS LTE 2100 MHz band and use ML regression techniques to predict its resonant frequency and directivity.
results: The CST simulation gives a reflection coefficient of −38.40 dB at 2.1 GHz, a bandwidth of 357 MHz, gain of 6.9 dB, directivity of 7.67, and efficiency of 89.9%; of seven ML models, linear regression achieved the best accuracy for directivity prediction and ridge regression for frequency prediction.
limitations: The authors explicitly state that they were unable to include deep learning models in the experiment; in addition, the antenna dimensions are relatively large (75 × 100 × 1.6 mm³).

title: Enhancing Circular Microstrip Patch Antenna Performance Using Machine Learning Models
ai_technique: KNN, Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost, GBR, LGBR (best: KNN)
core_claim: The authors design and optimize a circular microstrip patch antenna using six ML models predicting return loss (S11), aiming to speed up and improve the accuracy of the design process compared to classical EM simulators.
results: The KNN model achieved the highest S11 prediction accuracy (98.5%) among the six models tested (Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost, KNN, GBR, LGBR); the optimized antenna covers the 2.37–10.72 GHz band suitable for UWB, verified by fabrication and measurement.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors do not directly state limitations of the method, merely noting that the ML prediction agrees well with both the HFSS simulation and measurement for the given antenna; general transferability to other geometries remains unclear.

ai_technique: Review of ML/DL techniques (ANN, etc.) for antenna parameter optimization
core_claim: A review article describes the role of AI, computational electromagnetics (CEM), and ML/DL algorithms in antenna parameter optimization and gives examples of their use.
results: No original quantitative results; this is a review summarizing a number of works using ML/DL (ANN, SVR, combinations of evolutionary algorithms with ML) for antenna optimization.

ai_technique: Particle swarm optimization with variable-resolution EM models (variable-resolution surrogate)
core_claim: The authors investigate the benefit of incorporating variable-resolution EM models into nature-inspired (population-based) optimization algorithms for global optimization of antenna structures.
results: A suitably chosen profile of gradually increasing model resolution enables computational savings of up to 80% compared to optimization based solely on a high-fidelity model, without noticeable degradation of search reliability (verified with PSO on several antenna types).
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available text does not contain a discussion section with limitations; the method requires EM models sharing the same physical basis across the resolution spectrum, which limits its universal applicability.

title: Machine Learning Based Design of Pattern Reconfigurable Antenna
ai_technique: DNN (5-layer) with hyperparameters tuned by hybrid PSO-MGSA optimization
core_claim: The author uses ML (a five-layer DNN model) trained on a simulated database of 180 antennas to compute the resonant frequency of a reconfigurable antenna (RHBD) for 5G applications in the 26–29.5 GHz band, with the architecture and hyperparameters tuned by hybrid PSO-MGSA optimization.
results: The ML model was verified on a fabricated antenna operating at 27.5 GHz (trained on 162, tested on 18 samples from a database of 180 antennas); switching PIN diodes allows the radiation pattern beamwidth to be varied from 10.7° to 156.2°.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors do not give specific limitations of the model; the training database is relatively small (180 samples) and specific to the given antenna topology.

ai_technique: Q-learning (reinforcement learning) by an intelligent agent
core_claim: The authors propose the use of reinforcement learning (Q-learning) by an intelligent agent to control the radiation pattern of a massive MIMO antenna with beamforming, based on prediction of user movement within a 5G/6G network cell.
results: This is a concept/design paper without experimental numerical results; the authors describe a theoretical Q-learning framework (Q-function, states, actions, rewards) for modeling agent behavior in a cellular environment, but do not report specific quantitative simulation results.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the work is a purely conceptual/theoretical proposal without implementation or simulation validation of the proposed approach.

title: Machine Learning Assisted Hybrid Electromagnetic Modeling Framework and Its Applications to UWB MIMO Antennas
ai_technique: Multivariate Relevance Vector Regression (MVRVR) hybrid model; compared with SVR, GPR, ANN
core_claim: The authors propose a hybrid ML framework based on multivariate relevance vector regression (MVRVR), which solves both the forward and inverse EM problem simultaneously with a single model for UWB MIMO antenna design.
results: The HMVRVR model achieved a prediction accuracy (PE) of 94.32% for the two-element MIMO antenna and 98.93% for the four-element MIMO antenna, exceeding the results of SVR (63.98%), GPR (81.56%), and ANN (77.77%) in the two-element example.
limitations: The hybrid framework requires more computation time than separate forward and inverse models, due to the larger number of training samples and modeling parameters.

core_claim: This review article maps applications of machine learning and deep learning in the design, optimization, and selection of antennas for wireless communications, including surrogate and RL approaches.
results: Not stated explicitly: the article gives only a qualitative conclusion that ML/DL improves antenna behavior prediction, reduces the number of required simulations, and speeds up the design process, without aggregated numerical metrics.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors point to a lack of standardized antenna datasets and to the role of designer intuition in choosing network architecture and hyperparameters.

title: Machine Learning Approach in Optimal Localization of Tumor Using a Novel SIW-Based Antenna for Improvement of Ablation Zone in Hepatocellular Carcinoma
ai_technique: CatBoost, XGBoost, SVR, polynomial regression
core_claim: The authors propose an SIW antenna for microwave ablation of liver carcinoma and use ML algorithms to predict the ablation percentage based on probe position and tumor size, for optimal applicator placement.
results: The antenna achieved a maximum ablation diameter of 32 mm (transverse) and 25 mm (axial) at 20 W for 10 minutes; the CatBoost algorithm provided the best prediction (high R², low RMSE) compared to polynomial regression, SVR, and XGBoost.
limitations: The study is based solely on an FEM model without in-vitro validation; the model does not account for protein denaturation or deformation of liver tissue and the tumor during heating.

title: Machine learning technique based highly efficient slotted 4-port MIMO antenna using decoupling structure for sub-THz and THz 6G band applications
ai_technique: Random Forest Regression and other regression ML models (evaluated via R², MSE, MAE, RMSE)
core_claim: The authors propose a 4-port MIMO antenna on a polyimide substrate for sub-THz/THz 6G bands and use ML models to predict performance parameters (gain, efficiency) based on simulation data.
results: The antenna achieves a maximum gain of 15 dB, isolation >31 dB, efficiency of 97.40%, ECC<0.0012 at DG>9.99, and bandwidth of 85.71%; of five ML models, random forest regression (RFR) achieved the smallest error, with gain prediction accuracy >95% (R² and variance score).
limitations: Not stated explicitly in the available text (a conclusion is missing); given the breadth of the frequency band and the THz range, practical fabrication and measurement in this range may be difficult.

title: Modeling, Characterization, and Machine Learning Algorithm for Rectangular Choke Horn Antennas
ai_technique: Gradient boosting (current prediction) + neural network with Bayesian regularization (gain and HPBW prediction)
core_claim: The authors propose a hybrid model (analytical + FEM) for computing the far-field radiation characteristics of a novel rectangular choke horn antenna, supplemented by ML algorithms (gradient boosting and a neural network) to predict current values and radiation parameters (gain, HPBW).
results: The gradient boosting algorithm accurately predicted current values on the choke and reduced the need for repeated simulations in ANSYS HFSS; the neural network with Bayesian regularization achieved high prediction accuracy for HPBW and gain across various choke spacings; measurement at 2.45 GHz confirmed good agreement of the main lobe across methods (specific numerical errors are not stated in the extracted text).
limitations: The authors report differences in back-lobe radiation, primarily caused by reflections on the outer bottom side of the waveguide and by the effect of residual currents at smaller choke depths.

ai_technique: ANN (feedforward) for resonant frequency/bandwidth prediction
core_claim: An artificial neural network trained on the geometric parameters of a microstrip patch antenna can quickly and accurately predict the resonant frequency and bandwidth of satellite antennas without the need for repeated simulations.
results: The ANN (converging after 700 epochs) predicts resonant frequency and bandwidth in close agreement with both simulation (CST) and experiment, e.g., for one sample, 5.04 GHz (simulation) vs. 5.06 GHz (ANN) vs. 5.02 GHz (measurement); similar agreement was confirmed on four additional tested prototypes (L/S/C bands).
limitations: Not stated explicitly as a limitation: the authors mention that the work is so far only a software implementation (MATLAB), with hardware realization planned for later phases of the project.

ai_technique: SVM (Fine Gaussian) + ResNet (regression models, comparison)
core_claim: Combining classical machine learning (SVM) and deep learning (ResNet) enables efficient treatment of dual-band IoT antenna parameter design as a regression task, without the need for exhaustive manual tuning.
results: On a dataset of 1000 samples (700 training/300 test), Fine Gaussian SVM and Residual Neural Network (ResNet, various activation functions) models were successfully trained and compared for predicting the parameters of a dual-band T-monopole antenna; specific numerical accuracy metrics are not given in either the abstract or the conclusion.
limitations: The authors recommend expanding the dataset with a wider range of designs and using transfer learning to reduce computational cost; the current scope of data and models is thus limited.

title: An Artificial-Intelligence-Assisted Optimization of Imperceptible Multimode Rectenna
ai_technique: PSADEA - parallel surrogate-model-assisted hybrid differential evolution with GP regression for global antenna optimization
core_claim: The authors propose and experimentally verify a compact, optically transparent multiband/multi-range rectenna for wireless power transfer (near-field 13.56 MHz and far-field 980 MHz/2.45 GHz), whose geometry with 20 design parameters is optimized using AI-driven global optimization (PSADEA).
results: Maximum RF-DC conversion efficiency reached 55.45% at 980 MHz and 51.44% at 2.45 GHz (at a power density of 136.46 mW/m²), and even at a low density of 4.41 mW/m² remained at 21.81%/20.49%; inductive WPT at 13.56 MHz achieved a maximum system efficiency of 62.8% and a transferred power of 36.8 mW.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors do not mention limitations; the design is tied to a specific IFA geometry with 20 parameters and a specific type of transparent conductive film (TCF); general applicability to other materials/frequencies was not verified.

title: Antenna modeling based on meta-heuristic intelligent algorithms and neural networks
ai_technique: BPNN surrogate model optimized by metaheuristics (IBOAS, SOA, ARO, PSO, GA)
core_claim: The authors compare and improve several metaheuristic algorithms (seagull optimization, butterfly optimization algorithm with reverse learning, artificial rabbit optimization, PSO, GA) for optimizing the weights and biases of a back-propagation neural network (BPNN) modeling the behavior of ultra-wideband antennas.
results: The improved butterfly optimization algorithm with reverse learning (IBOAS-BPNN) achieves the highest prediction accuracy of 99.69%, followed by the seagull algorithm (99.51%) and the artificial rabbit algorithm (99.49%), compared to the classical PSO-BPNN (83.1%) and GA-BPNN (99.43%); IBOAS-BPNN also reduces the network's prediction time by more than 70%.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: in the conclusion, the authors merely state that a comprehensive, integrated system for this type of modeling does not yet exist and that optimization algorithms need further improvement; specific limits of their own method are not given.

title: On Accelerated Metaheuristic-Based Electromagnetic-Driven Design Optimization of Antenna Structures Using Response Features
ai_technique: Particle swarm optimization (PSO) with reformulation of the objective function using response features
core_claim: The authors investigate the benefit of reformulating the antenna design problem using response features instead of the conventional minimax formulation for metaheuristic (PSO) global optimization of multiband antennas.
results: The feature-based formulation smooths the objective function landscape and, under a limited computational budget, achieves better average objective function values as well as higher repeatability of results for all three tested antenna structures, with an overall average speedup exceeding 30% compared to the conventional formulation.
limitations: The authors report a twofold limitation: the method does not include a surrogate model, so costs can still be impractical for expensive EM simulations, and the approach requires easily identifiable characteristic points in the antenna response, which limits its applicability.

results: According to the abstract, comprehensive benchmarking on three antenna structures demonstrates the superiority of the proposed approach over both surrogate-assisted methods and direct nature-inspired optimization; specific numerical values of the computational cost are not given in the available text excerpt.
limitations: Not stated explicitly in the available excerpt (abstract, introduction, references); given the principle based on characteristic points, a limitation similar to the authors' related work can be expected - the need for easily identifiable resonance points in the antenna response.

core_claim: The article presents a vision for using AI (deep learning, reinforcement learning) to solve the NP-hard optimization of antenna (port) positions and precoding in fluid antenna systems (FAS/MIMO-FAS), demonstrated on an ISAC case study.
