core_claim: Integrating fluid antennas (FA) into the access point together with the proposed RDPG deep reinforcement learning algorithm improves the convergence and performance of an over-the-air federated learning system (OTA-FL) compared to fixed-position antennas and conventional DRL algorithms.
results: Simulations show that the FA system consistently outperforms both FPA and standard DRL algorithms (SAC, DDPG) in terms of average reward/OTA-FL convergence; performance increases with the number of antennas and decreases with an increasing number of users; specific numerical accuracy/speedup values are not given in the text (results are graphical).
limitations: Not stated explicitly: this is a purely simulation-based study; the authors do not mention specific limitations of the method in the conclusion.
source_file: old/107 - Enhanced Over-the-Air Federated Learning Using AI-based Fluid Antenna System.pdf

title: Emerging Computational Paradigms for Antenna Design in the Age of Surrogates and AI
ai_technique: Review: surrogate models (Kriging, neural networks, SVR), generative design (GAN/VAE/RL), evolutionary algorithms (GA/PSO)
core_claim: This review/bibliometric article analyzes the shift of computational paradigms in antenna design from population-based heuristics (GA, PSO) through surrogate modeling (Kriging, neural networks) to generative design (GAN, VAE, RL), including the geographic distribution of research.
results: This is a systematic literature analysis without its own experimental result; it identifies a trend of transition from evolutionary algorithms to surrogate and generative AI methods and regional differences in their representation.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: in the available text excerpt, no specific limitations of the review are given.
source_file: Emerging_Computational_Paradigms_for_Antenna_Design_in_the_Age_of_Surrogates_and_AI.pdf

title: Inverse Design of Microstrip Antennas Based on Deep Learning
ai_technique: CNN surrogate model + binary particle swarm optimization (BPSO) for inverse design of a pixelated antenna
core_claim: The authors propose an inverse design framework combining pixelated modeling of a microstrip antenna (10x10 binary matrix), a convolutional neural network (CNN) as a surrogate model for S-parameter prediction, and binary particle swarm optimization (BPSO) for automated generation of the antenna structure from required parameters.
results: A CNN trained on 150,000 simulations predicts S11 with RMSE 0.35, MAE 0.28, and R²=0.94; the BPSO-CNN framework converges faster and achieves lower S11 error at target frequencies than GA and simulated annealing; the CNN surrogate speeds up optimization of 500 iterations from 12.5 h (MATLAB-CST) to 30 min (25x speedup).
limitations: Not stated explicitly: for simplification of the initial framework, the authors assume an ideal lossless dielectric substrate, which is a simplification compared to reality.
source_file: Inverse Design of Microstrip Antennas Based on Deep Learning.pdf

title: A comprehensive review of AI and machine learning techniques in antenna design optimization and measurement
ai_technique: Review of ANN/DNN, decision trees, SVM/SVR, GPR, and metaheuristics (GA, PSO) for antenna design and measurement
core_claim: This review article argues that integrating AI/ML techniques (neural networks, decision trees, genetic algorithms, PSO) into antenna design, optimization, and measurement can significantly speed up and improve the accuracy of the process compared to traditional computational EM tools.
results: This is a qualitative review without a single unified quantitative result; it summarizes literature showing that AI/ML approaches achieve faster and more accurate antenna design solutions than conventional CEM tools (HFSS, CST, FEKO, etc.).
limitations: The authors state that the field is still at an early stage and that further research and development are needed to fully exploit the potential of AI/ML in antenna design.
source_file: A comprehensive review of AI and machine learning techniques in antenna design optimization and measurement.pdf

title: Advancements in Microstrip Patch Antenna Design Using Nature-Inspired Metaheuristic Optimization Algorithms: A Systematic Review
ai_technique: Metaheuristics: GA, PSO, DE, ABC, BFO, ACO, CS, FA, GWO, BA, IWO
core_claim: This systematic review examines the application of nature-inspired metaheuristic optimization algorithms (GA, PSO, DE, ABC, BFO, ACO, CS, FA, GWO, BA, IWO) to the design of microstrip patch antennas (MPA).
results: This is a qualitative review summarizing the impact of individual algorithms on bandwidth, return loss, gain, radiation efficiency, and miniaturization of MPAs, without a unified quantitative comparison across studies.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the review does not uniformly quantify the performance of individual methods and does not compare them on a common benchmark.
source_file: Advancements in Microstrip Patch Antenna Design Using Nature-Inspired Metaheuristic Optimization Algorithms A Systematic Review.pdf

title: Antenna Technologies for 6G – Advances and Challenges
ai_technique: Review of BPNN+GA, inverse ANN, RBFNN+SADE, and GRNN for antenna design/synthesis
core_claim: Artificial intelligence represents one of the key technologies supporting advanced antenna design for 6G, alongside hybrid arrays, reconfigurable surfaces, and THz antennas, particularly for surrogate modeling and inverse synthesis.
results: A review article with no original experimental results; in its AI section, it summarizes, e.g., a BPNN surrogate + multi-objective GA, an inverse ANN model for multi-objective antenna design, an RBFNN tuned by differential evolution (SADE) for phase nulling, and a GRNN for synthesizing excitation amplitudes of conformal arrays.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: this is a conceptual review of 6G antenna technologies; specific limitations of individual AI methods are not discussed.
source_file: old/101 - Antenna Technologies for 6G – Advances and Challenges.pdf

title: A Planar Array Synthesis Method Based on Deep Learning and Radiation Pattern Superposition Method
ai_technique: Dual-branch CNN with physics-informed hybrid training
core_claim: The authors propose a dual-branch convolutional neural network (CNN) with hybrid training combining data-driven and physics-informed approximation for real-time synthesis of multi-beam radiation patterns of planar antenna arrays.
results: The method achieves sidelobe suppression of 20-40 dB for two-beam steering over an off-axis angle range of 10°-20°; synthesis takes about 0.05 s (dataset generation ~0.7 ms per sample) and outperforms stochastic optimization algorithms and classical DNNs in efficiency and pattern regularity.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the text does not state specific limits of the method beyond the tested angle range (10°-20°) and the two-beam scenario.
source_file: A_Planar_Array_Synthesis_Method_Based_on_Deep_Learning_and_Radiation_Pattern_Superposition_Method.pdf

title: An Effective Antenna Shape Optimization Framework Employing Machine-Learning-Based Approach
ai_technique: Self-supervised-learning autoencoder (AE) + latent-space optimization
core_claim: The authors propose an antenna shape optimization framework based on a self-supervised-learning autoencoder (AE), which extracts latent features of antenna shapes and enables optimization with strong extrapolation capability at low training cost.
results: The framework was validated on a metasurface antenna and a transmitarray antenna; the fabricated transmitarray achieved a measured gain of 27.75 dBi (29 dBi in simulation) with an aperture efficiency of 42% and full 360° phase coverage using a single basic element, with processing completed in 13.9 h compared to tens to hundreds of hours for reference GAN/VAE/MAE methods.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the final structure requires manual selection by the designer with regard to simplicity and low manufacturing complexity; the framework was validated on only two antenna types (metasurface and transmitarray).
source_file: old/162 - An Effective Antenna Shape Optimization Framework Employing Machine-Learning-Based Approach.pdf

title: Antenna Design and Optimization for 5G, 6G, and IoT
ai_technique: Various (editorial summarizing the special issue; generally mentions AI/ML-driven antenna optimization across the contributions)
core_claim: This editorial for the Special Issue summarizes 11 contributions devoted to the design and optimization of antennas for 5G, 6G, and IoT, including MIMO arrays, near-field analysis of Vivaldi antennas, AMC structures, antenna array reliability, and AI/ML-driven optimization.
results: The individual summarized contributions report specific figures, e.g., a bandwidth of 2800 MHz and ECC<0.005 for a MIMO array, or an impedance bandwidth of 80.7% (1.7-4.0 GHz) with a gain of 7.0±1.0 dBi for a dipole antenna with an AMC reflector; however, this is an editorial without its own unified result.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: this is an editorial summarizing the special issue; specific methodological limitations are not discussed.
source_file: Antenna Design and Optimization for 5G, 6G, and IoT.pdf

title: Optimization of Antenna Array Configurations Using Deep Learning
ai_technique: FNN/Set Transformer surrogate for the cost function + gradient descent
core_claim: Deep neural networks (FNN/Set Transformer) can be used as a differentiable surrogate for the cost function of a sparse phased array, enabling gradient-based optimization of element positions and significant reduction of sidelobes.
results: The cost function (main-to-side-lobe ratio) for the ten best configurations decreased on average by 740% (range 699-816%) with penalization, and by 77% without penalization; the FNN surrogate achieved an MSE of 0.007 (Set Transformer 0.06) with inference of ~0.25 ms on an RTX 4070 Ti GPU.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the method was tested only on ten configurations with the lowest initial cost; broader generalization is not verified in the text.
source_file: Optimization_of_Antenna_Array_Configurations_Using_Deep_Learning.pdf

title: Physics-Informed Graph Neural Networks for the Inverse Design of GHz Reconfigurable Antenna
ai_technique: Physics-informed graph neural network (GNN)
core_claim: A physics-informed graph neural network (incorporating Kirchhoff's current law) can predict the RF switch configuration of a reconfigurable antenna from a desired current distribution for inverse radiation-pattern design.
results: The GNN achieved a switch prediction accuracy of 93.2% (TPR 87.97%, TNR 98.02%); reconstructed radiation patterns had an MSE of 0.001–0.0064 and an average current-distribution EVM of ~19.85 A/m for a sample of 10 random configurations.
limitations: The authors state that the architecture was optimized only for the given antenna size, validation was performed only in simulation, and scalability to other dimensions as well as real experimental verification are left for future work.
source_file: Physics-Informed_Graph_Neural_Networks_for_the_Inverse_Design_of_GHz_Reconfigurable_Antenna.pdf

title: Physics-Informed Deep Neural Network for Low Sidelobe Time-Modulated Antenna Array Synthesis With Harmonic Suppression
ai_technique: Physics-informed DNN (PIDNN) for TMAA switching sequence
core_claim: A physics-informed DNN can, without external training data, optimize the switching sequences of a time-modulated array to simultaneously minimize both SLL and SBL, significantly faster than evolutionary algorithms.
results: For a 16-element TMLA, PIDNN achieved a final loss value of 0.2721 dB after 250 iterations (53 s), whereas GA required 4,722,500 pattern evaluations (>2 hours) to reach a value of 0.5656 dB; GA achieved slightly better sidelobe suppression, while PIDNN was significantly faster.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors merely note that this is the first application of a PINN to TMAA synthesis, without discussing specific limitations.
source_file: Physics-Informed_Deep_Neural_Network_for_Low_Sidelobe_Time-Modulated_Antenna_Array_Synthesis_With_Harmonic_Suppression.pdf

title: Rapid Multi-Objective Antenna Synthesis via Deep Neural Network Surrogate-Driven Evolutionary Optimization
ai_technique: Branched DCNN surrogate + differential evolution (surrogate-assisted EA)
core_claim: A branched deep convolutional network trained as a universal (offline) surrogate for polygonal patch antennas enables fast antenna synthesis for arbitrary multi-objective specifications via surrogate-driven evolutionary optimization.
results: Antenna synthesis takes on the order of seconds, compared to hours to days for evolutionary methods with a full-wave solver; the fabricated wideband antenna achieved a maximum gain of 4.62 dBi at 2.65 GHz and a bandwidth of ~11.3% (below −8 dB), while the dual-band antenna achieved maximum gains of 4 dBi and 2.1 dBi.
limitations: The authors state that the approach requires more training samples than methods with a limited/reduced design space, which increases the computational cost of dataset preparation.
source_file: Rapid_Multi-Objective_Antenna_Synthesis_via_Deep_Neural_Network_Surrogate-Driven_Evolutionary_Optimization.pdf

title: Design of Sparse Antenna Arrays Using the Physics-Aware Generative Adversarial Network
ai_technique: Physics-aware GAN (PAGAN) with a physics-informed neural network (PANN), unsupervised learning
core_claim: The authors propose an unsupervised physics-aware generative adversarial network (PAGAN) method with a physics-informed module (PANN), which synthesizes sparse antenna arrays (element excitation, spacing, number of elements) to achieve a desired radiation pattern.
results: According to the authors, the method achieves the desired radiation pattern with fewer antennas, faster and more efficiently than existing methods; specific numerical values for speedup/accuracy are not given in the available text excerpt.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: in the available excerpt (introduction and references), no specific limitations of the method are given.
source_file: Design_of_Sparse_Antenna_Arrays_Using_the_Physics-Aware_Generative_Adversarial_Network.pdf

title: An Efficient Method for Complex Digitally Coded Antenna Design Based on Evolutionary Computation and Machine Learning Techniques
ai_technique: Ensemble learning surrogate model + genetic algorithm (SADEA framework) for digitally coded antennas
core_claim: The authors propose the DC-SADEA method (a surrogate-model-assisted evolutionary algorithm oriented toward digitally coded antennas) using ensemble learning surrogate modeling and genetic operators for the design of high-resolution pixelated (digitally coded) antennas.
results: The method was demonstrated on a broadband UWB antenna (~1900 pixels) and the feed network of a 5G base station (~1500 pixels); measurements confirmed the efficiency and effectiveness of DC-SADEA, but specific numerical improvements are not given in the available text excerpt.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: in the available excerpt (introduction and closing references/biographies), no explicit limitation of the method is stated.
source_file: An_Efficient_Method_for_Complex_Digitally_Coded_Antenna_Design_Based_on_Evolutionary_Computation_and_Machine_Learning_Techniques.pdf

title: An optimized surrogate model and algorithm with rapid multi-parameter processing capability for antenna design
ai_technique: TCN surrogate model + adaptive NSGA-III multi-objective optimization
core_claim: The authors propose a methodology combining an optimized surrogate model (Temporal Convolutional Network, TCN) with the adaptive NSGA-III algorithm for rapid multi-objective optimization of antenna parameters (S11 and gain) for a 5G mmWave antenna.
results: The proposed method reduces the mean squared error (MSE) by 12.489% and 3.277% compared to CNN and BiLSTM models, and shortens the computation time by 2.670% and 70.419% compared to the same models.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the conclusion only states that the method outperforms existing approaches in prediction accuracy and computational cost, without specifically stating limitations.
source_file: An optimized surrogate model and algorithm with rapid multi-parameter processing capability for antenna design.pdf

title: Deep Learning-Enabled Generative Antenna Design: Review and Prospects
ai_technique: Generative deep learning: GAN (conditional GAN), VAE for generative antenna design (GAD)
core_claim: This review article examines generative antenna design (GAD) using deep learning (especially GAN and VAE), which can overcome the limits of conventional parametric design thanks to a higher number of degrees of freedom and built-in prior knowledge.
results: The authors state that DL-driven synthesis can increase the gain bandwidth of a metalens antenna by 52% and reduce the sidelobe level (SLL) below -30 dB, with the possibility of full amplitude and phase control.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the text (available excerpt) discusses future challenges and trends in generative design rather than specific identified limitations of current methods.
source_file: Deep_Learning-Enabled_Generative_Antenna_Design_Review_and_Prospects.pdf

title: Filtering Antenna Design and Optimization by the Generative Machine Learning Approach
ai_technique: AC-GAN + threshold-moving random forest (TM-RF) classifier + evolutionary refinement
core_claim: The authors propose a generative ML framework (AC-GAN combined with a threshold-moving random forest classifier and evolutionary refinement) for automated inverse design and optimization of filtering antennas requiring simultaneous control of S-parameters and gain across multiple bands.
results: The proposed framework was used to design a compact filtering antenna, whose fabricated prototype shows close agreement between measured and simulated results; the authors report higher efficiency (fewer EM simulations), higher design quality, and robustness compared to conventional GA optimizations, without specific percentage figures in the available excerpt.
limitations: The authors state that future work will focus on improving training stability and computational efficiency, and that transfer learning could accelerate convergence and improve generalization across antenna topologies.
source_file: Filtering_Antenna_Design_and_Optimization_by_the_Generative_Machine_Learning_Approach.pdf

title: Physics-Informed Deep Neural Network for Low Sidelobe Cosecant-Squared Pattern Time-Modulated Antenna Array with Harmonic Suppression
ai_technique: Physics-informed DNN (PIDNN) with phase tuning
core_claim: A physics-informed deep neural network (PIDNN) with phase tuning can, without training data, synthesize a cosecant-squared radiation pattern of a time-modulated linear array while simultaneously suppressing SLL and harmonic sidebands.
results: For a 30-element TMLA, SLL of -39.54 dB and max. SBL of -42.27 dB were achieved; for a 40-element TMLA, SLL of -29.28 dB and SBL of -32.02 dB; PIDNN achieved the result in 2.87 min compared to 47.13 min for the genetic algorithm.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors do not mention specific limitations of the method in the available text; the results are simulation-only.
source_file: Physics-Informed Deep Neural Network for Low Sidelobe Cosecant-Squared Pattern Time-Modulated Antenna Array with Harmonic Suppression.pdf

title: Differential Evolution Based Manifold Gaussian Process Machine Learning for Microwave Filter's Parameter Extraction
ai_technique: DE-optimized manifold Gaussian process (Isomap + GPR)
core_claim: A method of differential-evolution-optimized manifold Gaussian process (DE-MGP) is proposed, which uses Isomap dimensionality reduction to address the problem of insufficient samples and high data dimensionality in surrogate modeling of microwave filters for coupling-parameter extraction.
results: The test error of the surrogate model was reduced to 0.84% for the fourth-order coupled filter and to 1.53% for the sixth-order filter.
limitations: The authors identify the risk of DE training of the manifold parameters becoming stuck in a local optimum, and the unresolved problem of a multivalued inverse surrogate model, as future challenges.
source_file: old/180 - Differential Evolution Based Manifold Gaussian Process Machine Learning for Microwave Filter’s Parameter Extraction.pdf

title: A Novel Deep Neural Network Topology for Parametric Modeling of Passive Microwave Components
ai_technique: Two-part deep neural network (DNN) + two-stage training
core_claim: A new deep neural network topology split into two parts (joint geometry+frequency processing vs. geometry only) is proposed for parametric modeling of S-parameters of passive microwave components with fewer training parameters.
results: The proposed two-part DNN topology achieves model accuracy comparable to a conventional fully connected neural network with significantly fewer training parameters; specific numerical values are not stated in the available excerpt.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the method assumes that the relationship of S-parameters with geometric variables is more complex than with frequency, which may not hold universally for all component types.
source_file: old/190 - A Novel Deep Neural Network Topology for Parametric Modeling of Passive Microwave Components.pdf

title: Inverse Modeling for Filters Using a Regularized Deep Neural Network Approach
ai_technique: Regularized Deep Belief Network (RBM + Bayesian regularization) for inverse coupling matrix extraction
core_claim: The authors propose a regularized deep belief network (R-DBN) for inverse modeling of coupling matrix extraction from S-parameters of microwave filters, combining unsupervised training using restricted Boltzmann machines with Bayesian regularization.
results: For a fourth-order filter, the model converges after 2039 steps with a training MSE = 0.0001; for an eighth-order dual-band filter (10000 training sets), the testing MAE on 200 new sets is 0.0018, with training taking less than 20 h compared to the days required for a deep MLP.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: a separate discussion of limitations is missing; the authors state that they present only two experiments, and broader applicability requires prior knowledge of the filter order and topology.
source_file: old/215 - Inverse Modeling for Filters Using a Regularized Deep Neural Network Approach.pdf

title: Double Deep Q-Learning Approach for Tuning Microwave Cavity Filters using Locally Linear Embedding Technique
ai_technique: Double Deep Q-Network (DDQN) + Locally Linear Embedding (LLE) for dimensionality reduction
core_claim: The authors propose a Computer-Aided Tuning method using Double Deep Q-Learning (DDQN) combined with Locally Linear Embedding (LLE) for dimensionality reduction, for automated tuning of microwave cavity filters.
results: The algorithm tuned a simulated ninth-order filter (four tuning screws) in only 23 steps, compared to 48 and 43 steps in compared works using PCA+DQN for fourth-order filters, and achieved a target return loss below -26 dB.
limitations: The authors state that the research focused only on the S11 characteristic (reflection parameter) without accounting for the filter's insertion loss, and that the distance from the target S11 value was used without accounting for side boundary lines for a sharper transition between stopband and passband.
source_file: old/211 - Double Deep Q-Learning Approach for Tuning Microwave Cavity Filters using Locally Linear Embedding Technique.pdf

title: A Novel Training Approach for Parametric Modeling of Microwave Passive Components Using Padé via Lanczos and EM Sensitivities
ai_technique: Neuro-transfer function (ANN + pole/zero) with MPVL and EM sensitivities
core_claim: A new training approach for parametric neuro-TF modeling of microwave passive components is proposed, using the matrix Padé-via-Lanczos method and EM sensitivities for reliable pole/zero pairing under large geometric variations.
results: The proposed training approach (MPVL + EM sensitivities) achieves better accuracy than existing methods under large geometric variations; computing EM adjoint sensitivities increases computational cost by approximately 10–20% relative to the EM simulation alone.
limitations: The authors state that computing the EM adjoint sensitivities required by the method increases the cost by approximately 10–20% relative to a standard EM simulation, although they consider this negligible relative to the overall cost.
source_file: old/191 - A Novel Training Approach for Parametric Modeling of Microwave Passive Components Using Padé via Lanczos and EM Sensitivities.pdf

title: Parametric Modeling of EM Behavior of Microwave Components Using Combined Neural Networks and Hybrid-Based Transfer Functions
ai_technique: Hybrid neuro-transfer function (ANN + pole-residue/rational TF)
core_claim: A hybrid neuro-transfer function technique combining pole-residue and rational transfer function formats is proposed, addressing the discontinuity and non-smoothness of poles/residues under large geometric variations in neuro-TF parametric modeling of microwave components.
results: The proposed hybrid neuro-TF technique achieves better accuracy than existing pole-residue and rational neuro-TF methods under large geometric variations and high transfer function order; specific numerical values are not stated in the available excerpt.
limitations: The authors state that the current method addresses pole/residue discontinuity at the same transfer function order but does not address the discontinuity arising from order changing, which they leave as future work.
source_file: old/195 - Parametric Modeling of EM Behavior of Microwave Components Using Combined Neural Networks and Hybrid-Based Transfer Functions.pdf

title: Comparative Analysis of Machine Learning Techniques for Temperature Compensation in Microwave Sensors
ai_technique: Comparison of classifiers/regressors (SVM, MLP, linear regression) + hyperparameter optimization
core_claim: Machine learning classifiers and regressors are compared for temperature compensation in a split-ring resonator microwave sensor, to recognize material type and concentration and extract ambient temperature from the frequency response.
results: Linear regression for temperature extraction achieved R² = 97% (10-fold cross-validation) and a mean absolute error of 0.58 °C; after hyperparameter optimization, MLP and SVM achieved classification accuracies of 0.97 and 0.99, respectively, for low methanol concentrations (1%).
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the model was validated only for methanol/acetone-water mixtures in the temperature range 25-50 °C; generalization to other substances or a wider temperature range is not discussed in the available excerpt.
source_file: old/187 - Comparative Analysis of Machine Learning Techniques for Temperature Compensation in Microwave Sensors.pdf

title: Improved Modeling of Microwave Structures Using Performance-Driven Fully-Connected Regression Surrogate
ai_technique: FCRM (deep regression surrogate) + Bayesian optimization of architecture/hyperparameters, performance-driven domain confinement
core_claim: The authors extend the performance-driven modeling paradigm with a fully-connected regression model (FCRM) for accurate and fast modeling of microwave structures (miniaturized couplers).
results: On two examples of miniaturized microstrip couplers, the method outperforms both conventional modeling techniques and nested kriging, with a reliable model built from only a few hundred samples.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the text of the available section does not contain a discussion of limitations; the scalability of FCRM to structures with a higher number of parameters (the curse of dimensionality mentioned in the introduction) generally remains an open question.
source_file: old/45 - Improved Modeling of Microwave Structures Using Performance-Driven Fully-Connected Regression Surrogate.pdf

title: A Novel Deep-Q-Network-Based Fine-Tuning Approach for Planar Bandpass Filter Design
ai_technique: Deep Q-Network (DQN) + NN forward/inverse surrogate models
core_claim: The authors propose a DQN-based approach for fine-tuning the structural parameters of planar microstrip bandpass filters with cross-couplings, using two neural surrogate models (forward and inverse) to significantly accelerate reinforcement learning.
results: The constructed DQN finds the optimal structural parameters of a fifth-order filter within 30 steps (examples: 26/29/18 steps), with a tuning time of about 0.4 s; training for 30000 episodes took 3 hours, whereas without the forward model it would be estimated to take 625 days.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: a separate limitations section is missing; the method requires prior accurate training of two NN surrogate models and was demonstrated only on one type of filter (fifth-order microstrip BPF).
source_file: old/204 - A Novel Deep-Q-Network-Based Fine-Tuning Approach for Planar Bandpass Filter Design.pdf

title: Machine-Learning-Based Microwave Sensing: A Case Study for the Food Industry
ai_technique: MLP classifier (FPGA/ARM implementation)
core_claim: A complete design workflow is presented, from microwave dataset acquisition to real-time hardware deployment of MLP classifiers for contaminant detection in packaged food.
results: MLP classifiers achieved "exceptionally high" contaminant detection accuracy; the two lightest models were implemented on an ARM CPU and an FPGA SoC for real-time operation; the specific numerical accuracy is not stated in the available excerpt.
limitations: The authors acknowledge that the approach was verified only on a specific case study (containers of hazelnut-cocoa spread); generalization to other scenarios and industries has not yet been experimentally verified.
source_file: old/188 - Machine-Learning-Based Microwave Sensing A Case Study for the Food Industry.pdf

title: Design of a Microwave Lowpass–Bandpass Filter using Deep Learning and Artificial Intelligence
ai_technique: ANN/deep learning for filter transfer function extraction
core_claim: The authors propose a compact dual-band lowpass-bandpass microwave filter, in which an artificial neural network and deep learning are used to extract the filter's transfer function for placing transmission zeros at the desired frequency.
results: The lowpass channel has a cutoff frequency of 1 GHz with insertion loss better than 0.2 dB; the bandpass channel operates at 2.4 GHz with an insertion loss of 0.5 dB in the passband.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the conclusion text does not contain a separate limitations section, only states that the desired results were achieved.
source_file: old/202 - Design of a Microwave Lowpass – Bandpass Filter using Deep Learning and Artificial Intelligence.pdf

title: Compressed Machine Learning-Based Inverse Model for the Design of Microwave Filters
ai_technique: LS-SVM regression + PCA compression (inverse model)
core_claim: The authors propose an inverse model combining LS-SVM regression with PCA compression to estimate the geometric parameters of a microstrip bandpass filter directly from the required scattering parameters, without iterative optimization.
results: The model was trained on L=200 samples with 9 geometric parameters and a 2002-dimensional input (S11, S21); the standalone LS-SVM model without PCA showed lower accuracy due to the high input dimensionality relative to the small number of samples, whereas the conventional ADS optimizer converged after 125 iterations in 3 min 24 s.
limitations: The accuracy of the standalone LS-SVM inverse model without compression was lower due to the high dimensionality of the input space (2002) relative to the small number of training samples (200) - the so-called curse of dimensionality; in addition, the inverse task is ill-posed (multiple geometry configurations may correspond to the same S-parameters).
source_file: old/172 - Compressed Machine Learning-Based Inverse Model for the Design of Microwave Filters.pdf

title: Machine Learning Assisted Novel Microwave Sensor Design for Dielectric Parameter Characterization of Water–Ethanol Mixture
ai_technique: ML regression predictive model (specific algorithm not stated in the excerpt)
core_claim: A new ML-assisted microwave sensor with two coupled spiral resonators (band-stop configuration) is proposed for characterizing dielectric parameters and the concentration of water-ethanol mixtures based on the stop-band frequency shift.
results: Numerical and experimental results show good agreement; the sensor (15×20 mm, 1.8 GHz) enables prediction of dielectric parameters/ethanol concentration using an ML model; the specific numerical prediction accuracy is not stated in the available excerpt.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the sensor was tested only for water-ethanol mixtures in a narrow frequency band (1.8 GHz); generalization to other liquids is not demonstrated.
source_file: old/181 - Machine Learning Assisted Novel Microwave Sensor Design for Dielectric Parameter Characterization of Water–Ethanol Mixture.pdf

title: Design of Terahertz InP pHEMT Using Machine Learning Assisted Global Optimization Techniques
ai_technique: SADEA – Gaussian Process surrogate model + differential evolution (DE) global optimization
core_claim: The authors apply the SADEA algorithm (surrogate model assisted differential evolution for antenna synthesis, based on Gaussian Process and differential evolution) for the first time to the design of terahertz InP pHEMT transistors, optimizing both material and structural parameters.
results: The optimized design achieved a 57% improvement in fT (from 220 GHz to 336 GHz) and an increase in fmax from 550 GHz to 770 GHz (an improvement of approx. 40%) compared to the commercial Diramics design, while maintaining a 100 nm gate length; the optimization took 16 hours on a standard desktop, compared to several days for conventional methods.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the work optimizes 16 design parameters, but the gate length was not optimized in this study; some optimized parameters reached the bounds of the search range, which may indicate a need for a wider search space.
source_file: old/176 - Design of Terahertz InP pHEMT Using Machine Learning Assisted Global Optimization Techniques.pdf

title: Artificial Neural Network for Dimensionality Reduction and Its Application to Microwave Filters Inverse Modeling
ai_technique: DR-ANN (dimensionality reduction) + multi-valued NN + DE optimization
core_claim: A dimensionality reduction (DR) strategy using an artificial neural network (DR-ANN) is proposed to simplify inverse modeling of microwave filters, combinable with multi-valued neural networks and differential evolution optimization.
results: The DR-ANN approach increased the robustness and convergence speed of DE optimization for coupling matrix extraction and waveguide filter optimization compared to direct DE optimization; specific numerical values are not stated in the available excerpt.
limitations: Not stated explicitly as a limitation, but the authors mention future extension to more complex (possibly multimodal) equivalent circuits of building blocks, suggesting verification only on limited examples (coupling matrix extraction, waveguide filter design).
source_file: old/183 - Artificial Neural Network for Dimensionality Reduction and Its Application to Microwave Filters Inverse Modeling.pdf

title: State-of-the-Art: AI-Assisted Surrogate Modeling and Optimization for Microwave Filters
ai_technique: Review: ANN/GPR/SVM surrogate modeling + PSO/GA/DE optimization
core_claim: This review article analyzes and summarizes the current state of AI-assisted surrogate modeling and optimization for microwave filter design, including data sampling, surrogate techniques, and advanced optimization algorithms.
results: This is a review article without its own experiment; it summarizes techniques for smart data sampling, advanced surrogate modeling, and optimization algorithms for microwave filter design, without specific numerical results in the available excerpt.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the article is a review without its own experiment; an explicit discussion of the limitations of individual methods is not included in the available excerpt (abstract + references).

title: A Microwave Filter Yield Optimization Method Based on Off-Line Surrogate Model-Assisted Evolutionary Algorithm
ai_technique: Offline ML surrogate model + evolutionary algorithm (YSMA)
core_claim: A YSMA method (yield optimization for filters using an offline surrogate-model-assisted evolutionary algorithm) is proposed, which builds a single accurate offline surrogate model that fully replaces EM simulations for global yield optimization of filters with more than 10 sensitive design variables.
results: The YSMA method was demonstrated on two real-world examples with 11 and 14 design variables and showed advantages over the dominant sequential online local optimization methods; the specific numerical yield improvement is not stated in the available excerpt.
limitations: The authors state that the search range for yield optimization is currently determined empirically, and plan a more systematic method for defining it as future work.

title: Parameterized Modeling Incorporating MOR-Based Rational Transfer Functions With Neural Networks for Microwave Components
ai_technique: Neuro-TF (neural networks + MOR-based rational transfer function)
core_claim: The authors propose a new neuro-TF method combining neural networks with a MOR-based rational transfer function, which addresses the "order-changing" problem and pole/zero mismatch without requiring EM sensitivities.
results: For a three-pole H-plane filter (49 training, 36 testing samples, EM simulation ~25 min), the model achieves training/testing errors of 0.69%/1.37%; for a fifth-order waveguide bandpass filter (81 training, 64 testing samples, EM simulation ~54 min), the error is 0.61%/0.78%, in both cases the lowest among the compared methods.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the method requires computing MOR (MPVL algorithm) for each geometric sample and was verified only on two types of waveguide filters.

core_claim: The authors propose a neuro-PTF technique combining a polynomial function with a pole-residue transfer function and neural networks, which increases the accuracy of parametric models of the EM behavior of microwave filters, particularly under large geometric variations.
results: The method is demonstrated on three application examples of microwave filters and produces more accurate models than existing neuro-TF methods; specific numerical accuracy values are not stated in the extracted text.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the text does not contain a specific discussion of limitations; the method requires a smoothness-discriminating algorithm to split subtransfer functions, which increases model complexity.

title: Automatic design of microwave filters by using relational induction neural networks

core_claim: The authors propose an unsupervised end-to-end framework called RINN (Relational Induction Neural Network), combining a clustering algorithm and deep reinforcement learning, for automatic design of microwave filters without prior engineering knowledge.
results: RINN successfully designed several second-order filters with different properties and one sixth-order filter; for the sixth-order filter it achieved a reflection loss of -10 dB, after which re-parameterization by the clustering module further accelerated optimization convergence.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: quantitative limitations are not specified in the text; the method is computationally demanding due to deep reinforcement learning training and was verified only on a small number of filter types.

ai_technique: Neuro-transfer function (neuro-TF) surrogate with adjoint EM sensitivity + trust-region gradient optimization
core_claim: The authors propose a new technique combining EM sensitivity with feature parameters (feature frequencies and heights) in a pole-zero neuro-TF surrogate model, accelerating gradient optimization of microwave filters and reducing the risk of becoming stuck in local minima.
results: The technique is demonstrated on three microwave filter examples and achieves further convergence acceleration compared to existing feature-assisted methods; specific numerical values for speedup or accuracy are not stated in the available text.

ai_technique: Deep neural network (forward model) for inverse design
core_claim: Deep learning enables inverse design of multi-port EM structures co-designed with circuits for fast and automated synthesis of mmWave passive elements and power amplifiers without the need for repeated EM simulations.
results: The proposed PA achieved a PAE of 16–24.7%, a saturated output power of 16.7–19.5 dBm, and a Psat,3dB bandwidth of 30–94 GHz (103.2% relative bandwidth), supporting single- and multi-band modulation at multi-Gb/s order.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available excerpt does not contain a discussion of limitations in the conclusion; the method is demonstrated on a 2D on-chip structure, with extension to 3D EM structures mentioned only as a future direction in the introduction.

ai_technique: Cascaded convolutional neural network (CNN)
core_claim: A cascaded convolutional neural network (CNN) model is proposed that, based on specified target characteristics, accelerates the design of planar microwave passive components and generates topologies different from classical templates.
results: The proposed cascaded CNN model was verified by EM simulation and hardware realization for matching circuits and power dividers, and generates topologies different from canonical templates; specific numerical accuracy is not stated in the available excerpt.

title: PACOSYT: A Passive Component Synthesis Tool Based on Machine Learning and Tailored Modeling Strategies Towards Optimal RF and mm-Wave Circuit Designs
ai_technique: Comparison of regression ML techniques (GPR, etc.) + optimization (PACOSYT)
core_claim: Regression ML methods combined with purpose-tailored modeling strategies are investigated for modeling integrated inductors and transformers, integrated into the PACOSYT tool for rapid synthesis of optimal RF/mmWave passive components.
results: The most accurate combination of ML technique and modeling strategy achieved an error below 2% relative to full-wave EM simulations and below 5% relative to measurements; the PACOSYT tool designs an inductor/transformer with optimal performance in the order of seconds and was used for automatic synthesis of hundreds of mmWave power amplifiers at 28 GHz.
limitations: The authors point out that none of the tested ML techniques provide good results without a carefully tailored modeling strategy for the specific design problem; generic use of ML without such a strategy fails.

title: Swarm Intelligence-Homotopy Hybrid Optimization-Based ANN Model for Tunable Bandpass Filter
ai_technique: ANN surrogate + hybrid homotopy-WOA/GWO metaheuristic optimization
core_claim: The authors, for the first time, combine a topology-based homotopy local optimizer with metaheuristic global algorithms (WOA, GWO) and an ANN surrogate model for the design of tunable microwave filters.
results: The ANN model serves as a surrogate to accelerate the hybrid optimization and to predict tunable circuit parameters at various stages of tuning; applied to a fifth-order tunable circuit and two EM simulation models of a fourth-order tBPF, with good agreement among computation, simulation, and measurement (specific numerical errors are not stated in the extracted text).
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the text mentions general shortcomings of existing methods (homotopy is only a local optimizer, WOA/GWO suffer from slow convergence and low accuracy), but specific limitations of the proposed hybrid approach are not stated in the available excerpt.

core_claim: The authors propose H-SMEAFO, a hybrid surrogate-model-assisted evolutionary algorithm for fully automated ('unsupervised') 3D optimization of microwave filter design without the need for designer interaction.
results: For an 8th-order dual-band waveguide filter, H-SMEAFO reduced the number of required EM simulations by approximately 30% compared to the reference SMEAFO algorithm (712 vs. 1211 EM simulations in the specific example); the total design took approximately half a day on a standard desktop computer.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: future work mentions the need for further acceleration of the method to reach the speed of an experienced designer; the method is demonstrated on only two specific waveguide filters.

ai_technique: Gaussian Process surrogate model + differential evolution (DE) global optimization
core_claim: The authors propose an AI-driven design methodology for MMIC power amplifiers (PA), combining a surrogate-model-assisted global optimization algorithm (Gaussian Process + differential evolution) with a data flow between the optimizer and the simulators.
results: The methodology was validated on a practical 3-stage PA design for 27-31 GHz with 30 variables; the design was successfully synthesized without an initial solution and met the specifications (matching <-11 dB, gain >16 dB, PAE >22%, output power >32 dBm) with good performance consistency across the band.
limitations: Not stated explicitly in the available text (a conclusion is missing); GP modeling has computational complexity O(N³) with respect to the dataset size N, which the authors address by limiting the training data to the 5d nearest designs.

ai_technique: ANN (MLP) surrogate modeling of S-parameters
core_claim: ANN models can accurately model and optimize the S-parameters of four types of microwave filters (LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF) in the 0–10 GHz band.
results: The ANN models achieved R² > 99% for all four filter types (LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF); for example, for BSF the accuracy on test data was R² 99.88–99.91% with MAE on the order of 10⁻³.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the model was validated only for four specific filter topologies on an FR4 substrate in the 0–10 GHz band; the authors do not discuss transferability to other substrates/topologies.

results: The proposed framework achieves competitive design quality with typically only about sixty high-fidelity EM analyses, compared to nature-inspired algorithms, gradient-based search, and other ML techniques.

limitations: Not stated explicitly in the available text (a detailed discussion of limitations is missing); future work mentions the need to validate E-GASPAD on more PA designs and to develop a software tool.

core_claim: The authors propose an adaptive homotopy continuation method that builds a database of geometric and electrical parameters (including tracking of transmission zeros) to train an inverse model enabling efficient geometry scaling of microwave filters.

core_claim: The authors propose a multichannel wavelet kernel network (MWKN) with two-phase PSO-based training for high-dimensional inverse modeling of microwave filters, using discrete wavelet transform to extract multiscale features from S-parameters.

results: The approach reduces the computational complexity of solving both the forward and inverse problems by orders of magnitude compared to conventional methods; experimental results from fabricated devices show good agreement with the method's predictions.

results: The AMID method successfully computed metasurface unit cells corresponding to the given EM properties (design targets), thereby validating its functionality and improving design efficiency; specific numerical accuracy metrics are not given in the extracted text.

core_claim: The authors present a three-stage ML-driven framework (global surrogate-assisted optimization, local refinement, direct EM optimization) for automated design of low radar cross-section (RCS) metasurfaces.

core_claim: The authors propose a metasurface-inspired wideband circularly polarized slot array with low RCS, whose feed network is optimized by the AI algorithm PSADEA (surrogate-assisted differential evolution).
results: The achieved bandwidth is 53.2% for |S11|<-10 dB, 50% for AR<3 dB, and 147.8% for the RCS reduction band; more than 5 dB RCS reduction is achieved over the 5-17.5 GHz band (111.1% relative bandwidth); the measured results agree well with the simulations and achieve the widest AR bandwidth among published low-RCS arrays.

ai_technique: Metaheuristic optimization (Jaya algorithm) of MTM structure parameters
core_claim: The authors design and experimentally verify a compact low-frequency metamaterial (MTM) structure for wireless power transfer, complemented by a metaheuristic optimization algorithm to lower the resonant frequency at which negative permeability occurs.

title: Physics-Informed Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Band Octagonal Fractal Frequency-Selective Surface Optimization
ai_technique: Physics-informed reinforcement learning (PIRL) with direct interaction with CST
core_claim: Physics-informed reinforcement learning (PIRL), interacting directly with CST, can autonomously design an octagonal fractal FSS with single- and dual-band response more efficiently than classical PSO.
results: For single-band OF-FSS designs, relative bandwidth errors of 0–6% versus the target were achieved (e.g., 106 MHz vs. a 100 MHz target); for dual-band designs, average errors of 5.8% (1.8 GHz) and 2.4% (3.5 GHz); compared to PSO, PIRL requires an order of magnitude fewer iterations (e.g., 42–169 vs. 430–700 for PSO) at comparable or better accuracy.
limitations: The work is purely simulation-based (CST); experimental fabrication and measurement of a prototype were not performed due to time constraints.

title: Machine Learning-Based-Inverse Topological Design Method for Active Frequency-Selective Surface (ITDM-AFSS)
ai_technique: MLP-mixer for inverse topological design (tandem/inverse network)
core_claim: The authors propose an ML-based inverse topological design method (ITDM-AFSS) using an MLP-mixer architecture, which maps EM responses in the ON/OFF states of a p-i-n diode directly to the pixelated topology of an active frequency-selective surface (AFSS) with a large number of degrees of freedom.
results: Relative errors between the target and predicted |S11| reach 0.3% (OFF state) and 3.8% (ON state); compared to a binary genetic algorithm (94 GB RAM, over 12 days of computation, errors of 3.2%/8.2%), the proposed method requires only 2.5 h of training, 11.7 GB of GPU memory, and 4.49 GB of RAM.
limitations: The authors state that minor differences persist between the predicted and reference structure, caused by the inherent nonuniqueness of inverse modeling.

core_claim: The authors propose an ML-based inverse hybrid design method (IHDM-MLFSS) combining topological and parametric modeling for designing multilayer frequency-selective surfaces (FSS) based on a given |S11| and number of layers.

title: Non-parametric Error Modeling for Ultra-wideband Localization Networks
em_category: Wave propagation, channels and communication systems
ai_technique: Nonparametric estimation of the error probability density + MAP optimization (gradient-less)
core_claim: Explicit modeling of antenna delay together with nonparametric estimation of the error probability density enables accurate and fast UWB network localization even under non-line-of-sight (NLOS) conditions.
results: A network of 8 UWB nodes (DecaWave DWM1000) achieved 3 cm RMSE accuracy under LOS conditions and 30 cm RMSE under NLOS conditions; explicit modeling of antenna delay reduced the RMSE from 6.2 cm to 3.0 cm in the best-conditioned LOS scenario; the entire localization process (up to 128 measurements/link) took under one minute.
limitations: The authors state that under high NLOS uncertainty the 8-node system is only marginally fully determined (28 equations, 24 unknowns), so under strongly NLOS conditions the antenna delay cannot be refined beyond its prior value.

title: Metaheuristic Optimization Techniques for an Electromagnetic Multilayer Radome Design
em_category: Wave propagation, channels and communication systems

core_claim: The authors propose a method for designing a multilayer electromagnetic radome (C-sandwich) using Ant Colony Optimization for continuous domains (ACOR), maximizing and stabilizing the transmission coefficient over a wide range of incidence angles in the X and Ku bands, and compare it with PSO.
results: ACOR converges faster than PSO (in the X band, 56.69 s/11 iterations versus 70.18 s/24 iterations; in the Ku band, 64.13 s/12 iterations versus 71.86 s/24 iterations for PSO), with both methods finding practically the same optimal design, and the optimized radome exhibits significantly more stable transmitted power over a wide range of incidence angles than an equivalent homogeneous radome.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors do not mention limitations; the design is limited to a specific five-layer C-sandwich configuration and specific frequency bands (X, Ku), and generalization to other configurations was not verified.

title: AI-based Satellite Ground Communication System with Intelligent Antenna Pointing

ai_technique: FI-GRU (RNN prediction) + II-DRL (Q-learning/DRL) for antenna pointing control
core_claim: Combining a predictive GRU model (FI-GRU) with improved deep reinforcement learning (II-DRL) enables more efficient and faster automatic pointing and tracking of a ground-station satellite antenna than the traditional Auto-Acquire algorithm.
results: In experiments, the azimuth fine-tuning time using FI-GRU and II-DRL was not significantly shortened compared to the traditional AA algorithm, but the elevation and polarization fine-tuning times with II-DRL were significantly reduced compared to both AA and FI-GRU (specific percentage values are not given in the text; the comparison is graphical).
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the work is validated only on a simulation test platform; the authors do not discuss specific limitations of the method in the conclusion.
source_file: old/103 - AI-based Satellite Ground Communication System with Intelligent Antenna Pointing.pdf

title: Optimization Modeling of the Hybrid Antenna Array for the DoA Estimation
em_category: Wave propagation, channels and communication systems
ai_technique: DNN-RBF vs. LS-SVM DDAG classification for DoA estimation
core_claim: The author compares two optimization methods for direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation with a hybrid (cylindrical) antenna array — LS-SVM DDAG and DNN-RBF — and argues that the DNN-RBF model overcomes the limitations of conventional (non-data-driven) methods in terms of accuracy and robustness to array imperfections.
results: The DNN-RBF model (5 hidden layers, 49 neurons) achieves approximately three orders of magnitude (10^3×) lower MSE — i.e., higher accuracy and robustness — for DoA estimation than the LS-SVM DDAG model; tested at a frequency of 10 GHz.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors merely note that the accuracy and robustness of DoA estimation generally remain sensitive to technological imperfections of the antenna array (calibration, mutual coupling, background radiation).
source_file: old/114 - Optimization Modeling of the Hybrid Antenna Array for the DoA Estimation.pdf

title: Adaptive modem and interference suppression based on deep learning
em_category: Wave propagation, channels and communication systems
ai_technique: Deep autoencoder (DAN) + CNN with transfer learning for interference suppression
core_claim: The authors propose an adaptive modem based on a deep autoencoder (DAN), supplemented with a single-convolutional-layer CNN for suppressing various types of interference using transfer learning, with the aim of replacing traditional MPSK/QAM modems and improving interference resilience.
results: The DAN achieves demodulation performance close to, comparable with, or better than traditional MPSK/QAM modulation across modulation orders up to 128-ary; the added CNN module, owing to transfer learning, converges quickly (within a few epochs) and effectively suppresses interference in both the frequency and power domains.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors do not directly mention limitations; they propose learning coding/modulation in a hybrid mode and receiver-side synchronization as future work, suggesting that these aspects were not addressed in this work.
source_file: old/30 - Adaptive modem and interference suppression based on deep learning.pdf

results: Not stated explicitly: this is a literature review without the authors' own experiments; the work compares and analyzes a wide range of ML methods used in the cited studies.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: as a review work, it discusses general challenges and future research directions of ML-enabled networks rather than specific limitations of its own method.

em_category: Wave propagation, channels and communication systems
ai_technique: Review of RL, DNN, and CNN techniques for communication and localization applications of antennas (beamforming, channel models, etc.)
core_claim: A review article summarizes the use of machine and deep learning across communication applications of antennas — millimeter-wave, body-centric communication, the terahertz band, and satellite, UAV, GPS, and textile antennas.
results: No original quantitative results; it summarizes the results of many cited works (e.g., RL for spatial beam selection in mmWave beamforming, DNN models for channel prediction, NN-DLL for multipath reduction in GPS).
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the text does not specify its own limitations, only summarizing the individual cited studies.

title: Machine Learning-Based Angle of Arrival Estimation for Ultra-Wide Band Radios
em_category: Wave propagation, channels and communication systems
ai_technique: Deep convolutional neural network (DCNN)
core_claim: The authors propose a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) for accurate angle-of-arrival (AoA) estimation in ultra-wideband (UWB) radio systems, robust to hardware imperfections, compared with the traditional MUSIC and PDoA methods.
results: The proposed method achieves a 99th-percentile AoA error of 2.8° compared to 180° for MUSIC and 84.5° for PDoA, i.e., an error reduction of up to 80° relative to traditional methods, with only a 10% increase in computational complexity relative to MUSIC; at N_CIR=5, it achieves 99th-percentile errors of ≤2.2°/2.8°/2.2° in clean, noisy, and multipath environments, respectively.
limitations: The authors state that the proposed method requires more training samples compared to the calibration samples needed for rule-based methods, and currently works only in a trained ("seen") environment - generalization to unseen environments is left as future work.
source_file: old/228 - Machine Learning-Based Angle of Arrival Estimation for Ultra-Wide Band Radios.pdf

title: AI-driven lightweight real-time SDR sensing system for anomalous respiration identification using ensemble learning
em_category: Wave propagation, channels and communication systems
ai_technique: Extra-trees (Extremely Randomized Trees, ERT) - ensemble classifier
core_claim: The authors propose a lightweight real-time system for identifying abnormal breathing based on contactless RF sensing using an SDR (USRP) and an extra-trees (ERT) ensemble classifier, supplemented by simulated data from curve fitting to expand the training dataset.
results: ERT achieved accuracy and F1-score of approximately 99% in classifying 8 breathing types, with a training time of only 0.71 s, significantly faster than a decision tree (82.25 s, ~98%) and a multilayer perceptron (163.94 s, ~99%).
limitations: The authors explicitly state that the data were collected only from five healthy volunteers simulating breathing patterns in a static position, because ethical approval for measurements on patients with an actual respiratory disorder was still under review at the time of the study.
source_file: old/230 - AI-driven lightweight real-time SDR sensing system for anomalous respiration identification using ensemble learning.pdf

title: Urban Navigation with LTE using a Large Antenna Array and Machine Learning
em_category: Wave propagation, channels and communication systems
ai_technique: Convolutional neural network (CNN) and k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) for channel fingerprinting, supplemented with a Kalman filter
core_claim: The authors demonstrate that channel fingerprinting with a large antenna array and CNN/kNN estimators in an azimuthal-delay representation of downlink LTE signals enables vehicle navigation in dense urban environments without line-of-sight to the transmitter, with meter-level accuracy.
results: The CNN estimator achieved a mean error of 6.2 m (5.7 m after Kalman filtering, max. error 35.9/12.1 m), the kNN estimator 10.4 m (7.2 m after filtering, max. 108.8/25.9 m); this represents an improvement over earlier works with median errors of 75 m and 19 m, respectively.
limitations: The estimates require knowledge of the user equipment (UE) orientation and clock synchronization with the transmitter; the measurements were limited by vehicle speed (max. 1.5 m/s) due to the channel coherence time, and the system was tested only on a single city block in Lund, which limits the generality of the results.
source_file: old/154 - Urban Navigation with LTE using a Large Antenna Array and Machine Learning.pdf

title: Extracting weak electromagnetic signals based on RMPSO-SPBSR in virtual anechoic chamber
em_category: Wave propagation, channels and communication systems
ai_technique: Regional Multi-role Particle Swarm Optimization (RMPSO) + Symmetric Piecewise Bistable Stochastic Resonance (SPBSR)
core_claim: The authors propose an adaptive symmetric piecewise bistable stochastic resonance (SPBSR) system, whose optimal structural parameters are determined by a regional multi-role strategy combined with particle swarm optimization (RMPSO), for extracting weak electromagnetic signals in a virtual anechoic chamber.
results: The proposed system achieves a correlation coefficient above 0.75 across a noise intensity range of 0-10 dB, effectively suppresses noise interference, and improves both the similarity and smoothness of the detected signal; specific numerical SNR improvement values are not stated in the extracted text.
limitations: The authors state that at higher noise intensity (above 10 dB) system performance degrades, and although reliability was verified on SSB signals, further experimental verification is needed for various modulation formats given the wide range of electromagnetic signals.
source_file: old/226 - Extracting weak electromagnetic signals based on RMPSO-SPBSR in virtual anechoic chamber.pdf

title: Intelligent Reception of Frequency Hopping Signals Based on CVDP
em_category: Wave propagation, channels and communication systems
ai_technique: CNN + Vision Transformer (dual multi-head Probsparse self-attention) - CVDP network
core_claim: The authors propose the CVDP network (CNN + Vision Transformer with dual multi-head Probsparse self-attention) for intelligent reception of signals with an unknown frequency-hopping sequence, in which the CNN extracts time-frequency features from spectrograms and the ViT module processes them with lower computational cost thanks to Probsparse attention.
results: Simulations show that the CVDP-based receiver achieves hopping frequency estimation and bit error rate (BER) close to a conventional receiver that knows the sequence in advance, even under various interference and fading channel conditions; specific BER values are not given in the available text excerpt.
limitations: In the introduction/conclusion, the authors implicitly assume that the hopping period and signal bandwidth are known and only the frequency sequence itself is estimated, which limits general applicability to scenarios with completely unknown hopping parameters.
source_file: old/33 - Intelligent Reception of Frequency Hopping Signals Based on CVDP.pdf

title: AI-Based Investigation and Mitigation of Rain Effect on Channel Performance With Aid of a Novel 3D Slot Array Antenna Design for High Throughput Satellite System
em_category: Wave propagation, channels and communication systems
ai_technique: Empirical/analytical predictive algorithms (ITU-R model) presented as an AI system
core_claim: A model combining three ITU-R-based predictive algorithms with real measured precipitation data can characterize and mitigate the impact of rain on a satellite link channel in the UAE, supplemented by the design of a 3D slot antenna array with frequency diversity for fade mitigation.
results: The rain-induced attenuation database for the UAE (10 years of data, 18–40 GHz) shows attenuation of up to 24 dB at 20 GHz (0.01% of the year, 33 mm/h rain rate) and up to 50 dB at 30 GHz in the rainiest year; SER reaches 0.9 for 64-QAM under extreme rain, versus 0.1 for 8-PSK; the proposed 3D antenna maintains a gain of ~22 dBi across the 12–40 GHz band for frequency diversity.
limitations: The authors recommend extending the analysis to additional modulation schemes and other geographic regions, and supplementing it with experimental measurements of satellite signal attenuation during rain for more robust validation.
source_file: old/106 - AI-Based Investigation and Mitigation of Rain Effect on Channel Performance With Aid of a Novel 3D Slot Array Antenna Design for High Throughput Satellite System.pdf

title: AI-Assisted Indoor Wireless Network Planning With Data-Driven Propagation Models
em_category: Wave propagation, channels and communication systems
ai_technique: Data-driven propagation surrogate (EM DeepRay) + AI optimizers (Bayesian optimization, population-based metaheuristics)
core_claim: The authors couple a fast data-driven wave propagation model (EM DeepRay) with an AI optimizer for automated planning of a dense indoor wireless network (UDN) according to a chosen KPI.
results: Optimization with the data-driven model finds the optimal small-cell placement in about 3 minutes versus more than 6 hours for ray tracing, i.e., a speedup of more than two orders of magnitude (two to three orders of magnitude overall).
limitations: The approach is limited to predicting signal quantities (path loss); modeling other channel characteristics (angle of arrival, delay) still has limited scalability, as the authors note in the discussion of future directions.
source_file: old/41 - AI-Assisted Indoor Wireless Network Planning With Data-Driven Propagation Models.pdf

title: Visual Result Prediction in Electromagnetic Simulations Using Machine Learning
ai_technique: CNN (convolutional neural network with residual blocks) for predicting surface currents from MoM/MLFMA
core_claim: The authors propose convolutional neural networks (CNN) that predict the image of the converged (accurate) solution of a scattering problem from images of surface currents obtained in early iterations of a MoM/MLFMA solver.
results: Relative tensor error norms between low- and high-resolution-error (LRE/HRE) images of 16.4-20.9% (NASA Almond) and 34.7-50.5% (Flamme), respectively, decreased after CNN prediction to 7.1-9.8% and 18.0-29.5%, respectively; a complete full-wave simulation of the Flamme geometry takes 7.6 h (100 iterations), while the HRE input for the CNN can be obtained in 18 min (4 iterations).
limitations: The authors explicitly state that the CNN model does not guarantee an accurate estimate at all locations of the current distribution and is prone to errors especially at sharp edges and corners, and that improving accuracy requires larger (computationally more expensive) training datasets.
source_file: old/262 - Visual Result Prediction in Electromagnetic Simulations Using Machine Learning.pdf

title: Characterization of electromagnetic parameters through inversion using metaheuristic technique
ai_technique: Simulated annealing with exponential/fast/Boltzmann cooling schedule
core_claim: The authors present a methodology for inverting electromagnetic parameters (electrical conductivity and permittivity of a vertically layered medium) based on simulated annealing, treating the scattered field as a global optimization problem, and study the effect of the Gibbs phenomenon on inversion accuracy.
results: The exponential cooling schedule achieves an error bound not exceeding 30% in the worst case across 10 tested two-layer models; after introducing Gibbs oscillation reduction, the error bound dropped below 25%, and accuracy improved relative to the original approach for 76% of the estimated parameters.
limitations: The authors explicitly simplify the problem by assuming that the thickness of each layer is known in advance (only permittivity and conductivity are estimated), and note the non-unique nature of such inverse problems.
source_file: old/264 - Characterization of electromagnetic parameters through inversion using metaheuristic technique.pdf

results: The article summarizes a range of ANN architectures (MLP, RBF, KBNN, neuro-TF, DNN) applied to filters, amplifiers, transistors, oscillators, and other microwave components; it is a review without its own new quantitative experimental results.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: as a review article it has no experimental limitations of its own, only a general mention of the cost of training data for complex, high-dimensional structures.

core_claim: This review article provides a comprehensive overview of machine learning applications in electromagnetics, particularly in antenna design, synthesis and analysis, electromagnetic inverse scattering, SAR target recognition, and fault detection.
results: This is a review without its own experimental results; it summarizes progress in ML for antenna design, synthesis, electromagnetic inverse scattering, SAR target detection, and fault detection in antenna arrays.
limitations: The authors explicitly devote a separate section to limitations (Section 4): the accuracy of ML algorithms depends on the availability of sufficient data and expert configuration of the model and hyperparameters.

title: A Maxwell's Equations Based Deep Learning Method for Time Domain Electromagnetic Simulations
ai_technique: Physics-informed neural network (PINN) for time-domain Maxwell's equations
core_claim: The authors propose an unsupervised physics-informed neural network (PINN) that encodes the initial/boundary conditions and Maxwell's equations into the loss function, thereby solving time-domain electromagnetic simulation without spatial/temporal discretization.
results: The method achieves accuracy comparable to FDTD (relative errors of the Ez, Hx, Hy fields on the order of 1.2-3.0×10⁻² for 2D scattering and multiscale problems) with a computation time of 0.65 s versus approximately 35 min for FDTD on the 2D scattering problem, i.e., a speedup of three to four orders of magnitude for multiscale simulations.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors do not directly mention limitations, but propose extension to the frequency domain and solving inverse problems as future work, suggesting that the current method is limited to time-domain forward simulations with a relatively small network (5 layers, 50 neurons).
source_file: old/260 - A Maxwell's Equations Based Deep Learning Method for Time Domain Electromagnetic Simulations.pdf

title: Electromagnetic Modeling Using an FDTD-Equivalent Recurrent Convolution Neural Network
ai_technique: RCNN (CNN+RNN) analytically equivalent to FDTD, without training
core_claim: The authors propose a recurrent convolutional neural network (RCNN) whose weights are analytically derived from the FDTD formulation, so that the network, without training, rigorously reproduces the FDTD computation on deep learning platforms.
results: RCNN-FDTD on GPU is 5.7 times faster than parallel FDTD on CPU; on CPU, RCNN-FDTD is conversely 1.9 times slower due to separate convolution computation for the E and H fields; accuracy matches conventional FDTD.
limitations: The authors state that a comparison with optimized GPU-FDTD was not performed due to the lack of a well-optimized GPU implementation of FDTD; this remains for future research.
source_file: old/59 - Electromagnetic Modeling Using an FDTD-Equivalent Recurrent Convolution Neural Network.pdf

title: An End-to-End Neural Network for Complex Electromagnetic Simulations
ai_technique: CNN + LSTM (end-to-end) with PCA dimensionality reduction, replacing FDTD
core_claim: The authors propose an end-to-end network combining a CNN (extraction of scatterer features) and an LSTM (prediction of the EM field distribution) with PCA dimensionality reduction, for a fast replacement of FDTD simulations in complex multi-scatterer scenarios.
results: The average relative squared error reaches about 8.05×10⁻⁵ for scenarios with a fixed number of scatterers and 2.25×10⁻⁴ for a random number of scatterers, with a computation speedup of about 1528x compared to FDTD.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors do not directly state limitations; the experimental section implies a restriction to 2D scenarios with simple geometric scatterer shapes (rectangle, circle, triangle) and a limited permittivity range, and generalization to 3D or more complex scenes was not verified.
source_file: old/273 - An End-to-End Neural Network for Complex Electromagnetic Simulations.pdf

title: A High-Quality Data Acquisition Method for Machine-Learning-Based Design and Analysis of Electromagnetic Structures
ai_technique: Iterative adaptive data acquisition (active learning) based on analysis of the distribution of existing samples
core_claim: The authors propose a high-quality data acquisition method for ML applications in EM structure design (antennas, metasurfaces, and other microwave structures), which iteratively analyzes the distribution of existing data and generates more informative samples instead of blind uniform sampling.
results: A comparative analysis of four implementations showed that the proposed method reduces the required amount of training data by approximately 40% while maintaining the same model performance, thereby saving approximately 40% of simulation and computational resources.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the article's conclusion is not included in the extracted text; the context suggests that the method still depends on iterative data generation using a full-wave EM simulator, which remains computationally expensive.

ai_technique: Review of ANN/DNN and other ML/optimization techniques in CEM (FDTD/FEM/MoM/PEEC)
core_claim: A review article maps AI applications (ML, deep learning, optimization algorithms) within computational electromagnetics (FDTD, FEM, TLM, MoM, PEEC) for modeling and diagnostics of power system equipment (transformers, electrical machines).
results: The article provides a comprehensive assessment of AI-based CEM techniques on case studies of electrical machine modeling, condition diagnostics, and design optimization, highlighting their advantages (reduced computational cost, faster convergence, handling of large-scale tasks); these are not quantitative results of the authors' own experiment.

title: Estimating Effects of Residual Physics with Machine Learning for Earbud Performance Prediction
ai_technique: Multi-fidelity fusion model (combination of simulation and experimental data) + k-medoids clustering for training point selection
core_claim: The authors propose a multi-fidelity ML method combining experimental data with FEA simulations (Ansys Maxwell + Mechanical) for predicting the sound pressure level (SPL) of a voice-coil speaker in wireless earbuds, accounting for uncertainties in material and physical parameters (residual physics).
results: The fusion model, using only 8 of 20 measured points (60% reduction in experiments), achieved high agreement with measurement (R² = 0.989); when reduced to 6 points (70% reduction) with the key point at 7 kHz, R² remained at 0.915; without this point, R² dropped to negative values, but the qualitative trend was preserved.
limitations: The authors state that experimental SPL measurements contain unquantified artifacts (inaccurate material properties, damping constants, mesh impedance at the aperture), and that with very sparse training data (6 of 20 points), R² can take negative values, although the qualitative shape of the curve remains correct.
source_file: old/216 - Estimating Effects of Residual Physics with Machine Learning for Earbud Performance Prediction.pdf
